items.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 59 (​OpenCost)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>OpenCost is an incubating CNCF open source project used for measuring and allocating cloud infrastructure and container costs in real time. Built by Kubernetes experts and supported by finops practitioners, OpenCost is a vital tool for operators to understand their Kubernetes spend footprint.

Join CNCF Ambassador Halil Bugol as he explored OpenCost for the first time.</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 58 (​Helm)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>A CNCF graduated project since 2020, Helm is an open source package manager for Kubernetes. It provides the ability to provide, share, and use software on clusters. It is a highly customizable tool, and it abstracts the application deployment to a simple configuration file.

Originally created in 2015, Helm is driving into 2025 with plans of a major new version, Helm 4, which has brought in new contributors and users who are passionate about application deployment on Kubernetes.

Join CNCF ambassador Satyam Soni as he explores Helm for the first time.</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 57 (​OpenYurt)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>A CNCF incubating project, OpenYurt joined the cloud-native ecosystem in 2020. This project is an intelligent edge computing platform which aims to extend the Cloud Native ecosystem to edge computing and IoT scenarios. By making non-intrusive enhancements, it empowers users to manage large scale edge computing workloads in different architectures (e.g., ARM and X86) in a native Kubernetes manner.

Join CNCF Ambassador Henrik Rexed as he explores OpenYurt for the first time.</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 56 (​Kubescape)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>A CNCF sandbox project, Kubescape was created by ARMO as an open-source Kubernetes security platform to provides comprehensive security coverage. It offers hardening, posture management, and runtime security capabilities to ensure robust protection for Kubernetes environments.

Join CNCF Ambassador Shivay Lamba as he explores Kubescape for the first time.</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff Episode 53 (​CubeFS)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>​CubeFS, originally developed in 2017 by JD.com (one of China's largest e-commerce companies) is an open-source, cloud-native distributed storage system designed to handle unstructured data at scale. It supports multiple access protocols, including POSIX, HDFS, S3, and its own REST API, making it versatile for various applications such as big data analytics, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), container platforms, and scenarios requiring the separation of storage and computing resources. ​CubeFS serves as a robust solution for organizations seeking scalable, high-performance, and versatile storage systems to manage unstructured data in cloud-native environments.​

Join CNCF Ambassador Swastik Gour as he explores CubeFS with you for the first time!</article>

As cloud-native development accelerates, observability is no longer a nice-to-have, but a necessity. This session explores key trends shaping the observability space, including the role of AI in transforming monitoring practices, the rise of open standards like OpenTelemetry, and how platforms like New Relic are adapting to meet the needs of developers and SREs to monitor traffic, microservices and cloud infrastructure or your AI/LLM integrations. Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Sponsored Keynote: Fluent Bit vs. OpenTelemetry Collector - Why Not Both? Margaret Dawson, Chronosphere

Fluent Bit and OpenTelemetry Collector are often seen as competing solutions—but that’s not the full story. Many teams use both, but figuring out when and how isn’t always obvious.
This session breaks down the top question we hear: When does it make sense to use Fluent Bit, OpenTelemetry Collector, or both?

Open source observability isn’t about picking winners—it’s about making the right tradeoffs for your needs. Interoperability gives us options, and options give us power. As vendors and contributors, our job isn’t to build walls—it’s to build bridges.</article>

Observability by Design: Leveraging OpenTelemetry Weaver To Take Control of Your Observability - Josh Suereth, Google & Laurent Querel, F5

Ever curious what labels are on a metric or trace, but can't find the documentation? Ever upgrade a service and find all your observability dashboards and alerts broken? Ever wish you had a tool to prevent developers or OpenTelemetry Collector config from breaking the signals you rely on?

In this talk we'll show how OpenTelemetry Weaver solves these problems. OpenTelemetry Weaver is the tool which powers OpenTelemetry Semantic Conventions.

This session will explore how to use OpenTelemetry Weaver to:
- Define your Observability signals
- Automatically generate documentation, code and tests, etc.
- Enforce compatibility guarantees
- Extended for your own needs</article>

items.title: Lightning Talk: Who Are You? Solving the Container ID Resolution With Cgroupv2 - Vincent Boulineau
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Lightning Talk: Who Are You? Solving the Container ID Resolution With Cgroupv2 - Vincent Boulineau, Datadog

In containerized environments, one key challenge of pushing observability data is to identify the sender, to allow data enrichment, i.e. attaching attributes, tags, metadata based on container or Pod metadata.
This problem has been commonly solved by asking the sender to provide its own container id, easily available through /proc/self/cgroup.

When Datadog started evaluating cgroupv2, it quickly became apparent that this approach was not usable anymore, prompting us to find new solutions as the most common workaround (using mountinfo) is actually not reliable.
In this talk we'll explain why the container id is not available anymore (due private cgroup namespace) and present two solutions that we worked on.

We will go through the benefits of each one and will share how these solutions could be used in other observability projects, like OpenTelemetry.</article>

OpenTelemetry at Delivery Hero: The Good, the Bad and the Vendor-Agnostic - Elena Kovalenko, Delivery Hero

In this session I’m going to take you on a journey of Delivery Hero - an international food delivery company - towards the goal of becoming observability vendor-independent. This journey will be long and hard and will take our hero through the forest of the unstable OpenTelemetry contrib components and the swamp of high memory and CPU consumption. It will require them to find new allies to overcome the challenge of routing for stateful collector components in non-federated environments and fight the metric temporality conversion monsters. I am going to demonstrate what keeps our hero motivated after all these hurdles and why they are still convinced that OpenTelemetry is the right tool for them to accomplish this mission.</article>

Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

From Legacy Vendor Tooling To OTEL: Scaling Observability at MSCI With a Four-Person Team - Aftab Khan & Zach Arnold, MSCI


Migrating observability infrastructure for a 5,000-person financial services company is daunting enough - doing it with just four engineers might seem impossible. This session details MSCI's journey from a traditional legacy tooling infrastructure to a modern cloud-native observability stack built on OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, Jaeger, Grafana, and Elasticsearch. We'll share our architectural decisions, implementation strategy, and critical lessons learned while maintaining observability during the transition. Through real-world examples, we'll demonstrate how we overcame scaling challenges, managed the cultural shift, and achieved better visibility while significantly reducing costs. Learn practical strategies for planning your own observability migration, including how to phase the transition, train teams effectively, and avoid the pitfalls we encountered.</article>

In some high-security or compliance-regulated environments, we sometimes need to guarantee that data is encrypted while in transit. In this talk, we will show you how to accomplish this using existing off-the-shelf OpenTelemetry components. We will walk you through the detailed technical process generating certificates and configuring the Collector to receive encrypted telemetry from the OpenTelemetry Java Instrumentation agent.</article>

items.title: From Chaos To Clarity: Scaling Observability at Dropbox With Centralized Logging Solu... Alok Ranjan

Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

From Chaos To Clarity: Scaling Observability at Dropbox With Centralized Logging Solution - Alok Ranjan, Dropbox Inc

At Dropbox, managing observability for systems producing terabytes of logs daily posed a unique challenge. Initially, developers accessed logs by logging into individual servers, a process further complicated when we moved to containers. Containers are short-lived, which caused logs to disappear on termination. This highlighted the need for a scalable, persisted, centralized solution using open-source tools.

In this session, I’ll discuss our journey to build a robust observability framework centered on Loki as our logging solution. Scaling Loki to Dropbox’s data volume required extensive optimizations for reliable, efficient query performance. I’ll cover our deployment, challenges, and strategies for achieving high-performance logging.

We also integrated Grafana to unify logs and metrics in a single view, enhancing troubleshooting and security. Join us to learn how Dropbox scaled its observability with open-source solutions and key lessons from our experience.</article>

Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Manage Cloud Native LLM Workloads Across Edge and Cloud Seamlessly Using KubeEdge and WasmEdge - Vivian Hu, Second State & Fei Xu, Huawei Cloud

LLMs moving beyond data centers to edge devices. While this migration promises reduced latency and enhanced privacy, challenges come: maintaining accuracy within limited resources, and cross-device deployment problems.

The integration of KubeEdge and WasmEdge addresses the challenge. WasmeEdge is a lightweight, portable runtime (less than 50MB) without external dependencies. The KubeEdge Sedna orchestrates the edge-cloud collaboration. It monitors inference accuracy and automatically routes requests to cloud-based models when edge processing doesn't meet accuracy thresholds.

This session will demo that small LLMs provide quick, local inference at the edge. When higher accuracy is needed, Sedna seamlessly transitions to larger models in the cloud. The inference workload is built in Rust and compiled to Wasm, enabling deployment across edge and cloud without any changes.

The solution has been implemented in production across multiple industries like aerospace and bank branches.</article>

Lightning Talk: From HAR To OpenTelemetry Trace: Redefining Your Observability - Antonio Jimenez Martinez, Cisco ThousandEyes

Have you heard about HTTP Archive (HAR) files and wondered how you could leverage this data for deeper insights into your web applications?

Imagine analyzing your page load request data as OpenTelemetry traces in your favorite observability backend. In this talk, we will explore the lessons learned from transforming HAR into an OpenTelemetry trace and streaming it to Jaeger.

You'll gain insights into the process of converting HAR data into spans following OpenTelemetry semantic conventions, and learn about the architecture we used to send these traces to any observability backend via the OpenTelemetry collector. This session is perfect for developers and observability engineers looking to enrich their tracing capabilities with detailed HTTP request data.</article>

items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Cluster Management for Large Scale AI and GPUs: Challenges and Opportunities - Claudia Misale & David Grove, IBM

There are new challenges in managing large GPU clusters dedicated to cloud native AI workloads. The workload mix is diverse, and GPUs must be effectively utilized and dynamically shared across multiple teams. Furthermore, GPUs are subject to a variety of performance degradations and faults that can severely impact multi-GPU jobs, thus requiring continuous monitoring and enhanced diagnostics. Cloud native tools such Kubeflow, Kueue and others, are the building blocks for large scale GPU clusters used by teams across IBM Research for training, tuning, and inference jobs. In this talk, IBM Research will share and demonstrate lessons learnt on how they configure large scale GPU clusters and the development of Kubernetes native automation to run health checks on GPUs and report health. Finally, will show the use of diagnostics to enable both the dynamic adjustment of quotas to account for faulty GPUs, and the automatic steering of new and existing workloads away from nodes with faulty GPUs.</article>

items.title: Symbiotic Security Unveils AI Coding Tool Trained to Identify Vulnerabilities
items.author: Mike Vizard

items.title: Announcing General Availability of GitLab Duo with Amazon Q
items.author: Ryan Bachman
items.content:
Today, we’re excited to announce the general availability of GitLab Duo with Amazon Q. This new offering is an integrated product, bringing together GitLab’s DevSecOps platform with Amazon Q’s generative AI capabilities. Gitlab Duo with Amazon Q embeds Amazon Q agent capabilities directly in GitLab’s DevSecOps platform to accelerate complex, multi-step tasks across the entire software development lifecycle.

In today’s fast-paced software development environment, developers are constantly looking for ways to improve productivity while maintaining best practices for code quality, security, and deployment. The integration of GitLab Duo with Amazon Q addresses these needs by combining GitLab’s comprehensive DevSecOps platform with Amazon Q’s intelligent coding assistance.

This integration enables developers to leverage AI throughout their entire workflow—from idea conception to deployment—all within the familiar GitLab environment they already use. For new and existing Amazon Q Developer users, this integration also leverages the same Q Developer agents available in the IDE, providing a consistent experience across different interfaces.

Key Benefits and Features

The GitLab Duo with Amazon Q integration delivers significant value to development teams by creating a more efficient, secure, and collaborative workflow.

This integration eliminates the need to switch between different tools and environments, as developers can access powerful AI assistance directly within GitLab. GitLab helps automate building, testing, packaging, and deployment of secure code, streamlining the entire development lifecycle. What makes this particularly powerful is how the AI agents utilize the context throughout a GitLab project to keep the SDLC “loop” going. So whether you are troubleshooting a failed pipeline, investigating a vulnerability, or writing a new feature, Amazon Q agents can leverage the appropriate context to assist you with the task at hand.

Security and compliance are foundational elements of this integration. End-to-end security controls are built directly into the platform. The Amazon Q agents come with appropriate guardrails to help customers meet compliance without affecting development velocity, all while leveraging AWS’s cloud infrastructure to scale your AI-enhanced development workflows with confidence. You can ask Amazon Q agents to help remediate a finding in the project’s vulnerability reports or help troubleshoot a failed pipeline.

Throughout your development workflow, you’ll find collaborative AI agents ready to assist with various tasks. Whether you need to upgrade Java code from version 8 or 11 to 17, get AI-powered code review suggestions, automatically generate comprehensive test cases, or transform ideas into complete merge requests—Amazon Q is there to help at every step. These intelligent agents work alongside your team, enhancing productivity.

To demonstrate how GitLab and Amazon Q complement each other to accelerate development productivity and help organizations with application security, I’ll be using a Java application enjoyed by puzzle enthusiasts.

Whether you are looking to scale your developer teams or streamline the processes between feature requests and production, GitLab Duo with Amazon Q is now integrated into GitLab’s platform, so you can begin development simply by assigning a GitLab issue to Amazon Q Developer agents.

I start by creating a task in my GitLab project. I want to create a new feature to support multiple languages in the Q words game.

From here I assign the task directly to the Amazon Q agent by using GitLab’s quick action in the issue’s comment section.

The agent will automatically open up a merge request for me to review with suggested code changes. Here you can see changes the agent made across 11 files, accounting for front-end, API, and styling changes. In the past I would have opened my IDE, cloned the project, and coded these changes myself. Using GitLab Duo with Amazon Q, I just review and test the new code before I am ready to deploy.

Code reviews play a critical function during the development life-cycle. They act as a quality gate to help maintain high quality security and coding standards. While important, code reviews add latency to software delivery, especially when reviewers are not available or when changes are complex.

The Amazon Q agent for Code Reviews in GitLab helps teams move faster through their code review. Using the quick action in a merge request comment field sends the merge request to Amazon Q, where it will identify security and quality risks associated with code changes in the merge request.

I start by opening an open merge request. In this example, another developer had the task to add authentication to the Q words application.

I then invoke the agent with the quick action.

The review is returned as inline code suggestions to the merge request. Here you can see an example of a finding from the review agent. Comments include a description of the findings as well as guidance and links to help improve the code.

I next use the Gitlab Duo with Amazon Q chat agent in the web interface to ask for a summary of the change and ask it to highlight any critical issues. GitLab Duo chat allows me to ask questions about the current resource in the URL. In this example it is the merge request, but it could also be a GitLab issue I want to explain or a summary of a code file in a repository.

Next, I ask GitLab Duo with Amazon Q to generate tests using the quick action. Adding this action to the comment field will generate recommended tests when the MR lacks sufficient tests.

The summary I receive from GitLab Duo with Amazon Q helps me understand the scope of the changes and focuses my attention to the more important aspects of the change. Along with the tests that Q Developer agents recommended, I am able to approve the merge request in less time.

Upgrading Java applications from older versions to Java 17 can be time-consuming and error-prone. With GitLab Duo and Amazon Q, I can leverage the transform agent to help me automate the migration from the current Java 8 code to Java 17 along with upgrading the project’s dependencies. I start by creating a new issue in my GitLab project that indicates the Java upgrade.

To begin the upgrade, I use the GitLab Q quick action to begin the upgrade process. The Amazon Q transformation agent asks me to update the gitlab-ci.yaml file to continue the process.

I can follow the agent’s progress by watching for updates in the Issue’s details. GitLab Duo with Amazon Q will also add a transformation plan to the issue so I can understand what types of changes will be involved to complete the upgrade.

When the transform is complete, a new merge request is opened for me to review. As you can see, my pom.xml file was updated to compile on Java 17 as well as additional changes to ensure the project compiles. It also includes a report detailing next steps to consider before merging and deploying the updated Java code.

In this post, I demonstrated how GitLab Duo with Amazon Q can help scale and improve application development. Using GitLab Duo with Amazon Q, I was able to quickly add additional features, review code changes, and upgrade my application to Java 17 all within GitLab’s collaborative interface. I now have a secure and modern java app that I can use to practice my Español.

The general availability of GitLab Duo with Amazon Q marks a significant milestone in AI-assisted software development. By combining GitLab’s comprehensive DevSecOps platform with Amazon Q ‘s generative AI capabilities, this integration empowers development teams to work more efficiently while maintaining high standards of security and compliance.

Organizations can now leverage this powerful integration to accelerate their software development lifecycle, reduce manual effort, and ship more secure code faster. The seamless developer experience, enterprise-grade security, and collaborative AI agents throughout the workflow make this integration a valuable addition to any development team’s toolkit. We’re excited to see how customers leverage this integration to transform their development processes and achieve new levels of productivity and innovation.

Lightning Talk: Scheduling Success: Precision Updates for Continuous Manufacturing Operations - Juan Camilo Orozco, Bosch Connected Industry

Cloud-native technologies are gaining traction in manufacturing, as the industry strives for zero-downtime deployments in production systems. However, many plants rely on legacy software that doesn’t integrate smoothly with cloud-native environments. Even when containerized, these components often fail to support seamless request redirection between replicas, causing disruptions during cluster or node updates. These disruptions require precise scheduling around plant shift plans. This lightning talk will explain why maintenance windows can still be effective and how Bosch Connected Industry addresses the limitations of public cloud providers' update controls. You’ll learn a simple yet effective approach to managing cluster updates and node image promotions in production-critical environments.</article>

items.title: C.A.L.L.I.N.G. Now I'm Calling You, Calling You Now - Mario Macías & Terra Tauri, Grafana Labs
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

C.A.L.L.I.N.G. Now I'm Calling You, Calling You Now - Mario Macías & Terra Tauri, Grafana Labs

The Kubernetes API is awesome and so tempting to use, especially when building Observability Solutions. Nobody wants to just get raw IP addresses and ports in their network or request telemetry, it’s much better to see your pod and service metadata. But what’s even better is that getting information about all the nodes in your cluster can help you produce amazing service graphs.

This talk is a story of how we took down the Kubernetes API in our biggest production cluster at Grafana, by deploying observability tools which make heavy use of the Kubernetes API. We’ll show you the techniques we used to avoid repeating our mistakes, by applying configuration changes and building services which helped us shield the Kubernetes API from the information thirsty observability tools, while keeping the functionality intact.</article>

items.title: Accelerate large-scale modernization of .NET, mainframe, and VMware workloads using Amazon Q Developer
items.author: Krishna Parab
items.content:
Software runs the world – not just the new software applications built in modern languages and deployed on the most optimized cloud infrastructure, but also legacy software built over years and barely understood by the teams that inherit them. These legacy applications may have snowballed into monolithic blocks or may be fragmented across siloed on-premises infrastructure. The significant maintenance, security, and compliance challenges caused can create lasting implications for business performance and competitiveness. Therefore, transformation of legacy applications using modern languages, new frameworks, and cloud services has become an organizational imperative.

Application modernization challenges

Modernization of software applications is a long and painful journey – requiring large teams of developers, domain experts, and consultants who first need to understand the application landscape, devise strategic modernization plans, and then tactically implement the plans in phases, typically over a span of many years. This process is linear, slow, and complex. Traditional labor-intensive modernization approaches incur significant costs and take years to leverage new cloud technologies and innovations for business-critical applications.

Generative AI can help with intelligent automation, domain expertise, and scalability to transform modernization journeys.

Introducing Amazon Q Developer transformation capabilities

Amazon Q Developer transformation capabilities

Amazon Q Developer, the most capable generative AI–powered assistant for software development, is now the first generative AI-powered assistant for large-scale modernization and migration of .NET, mainframe, and VMware workloads. This extends Q Developer’s transformation capabilities for Java upgrades launched in April 2024 to new types of workloads. Q Developer combines both foundational models and specialized tools based on AI and automated reasoning via that tackle workload-specific modernization steps spanning analysis, planning, and implementation.

Multifunctional teams, including consultants, IT experts, workload domain experts, and developers, can use a to offload transformation tasks to Amazon Q Developer agents and transform hundreds of workloads at a time. The agents can port .NET Framework to cross-platform Linux-ready .NET, modernize COBOL applications on mainframes to Java applications on AWS, or virtualized workloads on VMware to scalable workloads on EC2. The modernization teams engage with Q Developer using natural language and share transformation objectives, code repositories, and context. Q Developer agents analyze artifacts like code segments, dependencies, and integrations, applying expertise from prior modernizations. They propose customized plans tailored to codebases, resource utilization, and objectives. The teams can then review, adjust, and approve the plans with iterative engagement with the agents. After the plans are approved, the agents implement the transformation keeping the modernization teams updated on milestones completed and blockers needing human guidance. The transformation journey is an interactive process between the modernization team and Q Developer, with modernization team maintaining control and visibility over the transformation.

Natural language chat with Q Developer AI agents

Faster, scalable, and better modernization

Amazon Q Developer enhances transformation in three primary ways – acceleration, scalability, and quality.

Amazon Q Developer automates and accelerates complex, multi-step processes. Agents conduct assessment and discovery of legacy artifacts to build documentation and dependency maps that improve the understanding of source assets. Most large-scale modernization projects are done in waves that need to be carefully planned. The agents develop modernization wave plans based on source dependencies, stated project goals, and teams can review and approve the plans. Thereafter, the goal-seeking autonomous agents handle implementation complexities to execute the plans. Customers using Amazon Q Developer can modernize Windows .NET applications to Linux up to four times faster than traditional methods and help customers realize up to 40% savings in licensing costs. for the sequence to transform monolith z/OS COBOL application code that takes months to accomplish with human subject matter experts, Amazon Q Developer generates . Q Developer agents convert on-premises VMware network configurations into modern AWS equivalents in hours vs. the weeks required with traditional manual approaches. The shorter time spent on manual modernization means more freedom for your team to focus on innovation.

Modernization has traditionally been a linear journey with multiple steps and dependencies on cross-functional teams with limited mechanisms for collaboration. This limits teams’ ability to tackle large-scale projects. Amazon Q Developer addresses the challenges by task parallelization and web-based collaboration. Multiple generative AI agents work simultaneously on tasks. Large monolithic applications can be decomposed along business functions like engineering, marketing, sales applications, and transformed in parallel. A unified web-based experience for large-scale transformation means multi-functional team members can collaborate with the autonomous agents, and review and approve key decisions in one place, enabling teams to execute larger and more complex projects in a given time.

Finally, the quality of transformation manifested in functional equivalence, security, and resilience of modernized applications determines the business outcomes like project ROI and operational performance. To ensure transformation quality, you need expertise in languages and frameworks like COBOL, Java, .NET; specialized steps like code base analysis, monolith decomposition, code refactoring, network translation; and domains like mainframe, virtualization, and cloud. You may not have the requisite expertise in your team. That is where Amazon Q Developer can help. Q Developer agents are trained with specific domain expertise to identify code dependencies and frameworks, replace deprecated code, upgrade to new language frameworks, incorporate security best practices, and validate upgraded workloads using workload-tailored plans. Your team can examine the agents’ recommendations, make informed decisions, and guide the modernization journey towards better outcomes like enhanced security, compliance, and performance.

Workloads supported by Amazon Q Developer transformation capabilities

Amazon Q Developer transformation capabilities are now available in preview. To learn more, please visit Q Developer web page featuring short demo videos and documentation that can get you started. Read the AWS News blogs that walk you through the unified web experience and IDE experience. Dive deeper into the transformation of specific workloads by reading the workload-specific blogs related to transformation of .NET, mainframe, and VMware workloads.

items.title: Docker Desktop for Mac: QEMU Virtualization Option to be Deprecated in 90 Days
items.author: Kat Tomrushka
items.content:
We are announcing the upcoming deprecation of QEMU as a virtualization option for Docker Desktop on Apple Silicon Macs. After serving as our legacy virtualization solution during the early transition to Apple Silicon, QEMU will be fully deprecated 90 days from today, on July 14, 2025. This deprecation does not affect QEMU’s role in emulating non-native architectures for multi-platform builds. By moving to Apple Virtualization Framework or Docker VMM, you will ensure optimal performance.

Why We’re Making This Change

Our telemetry shows that a very small percentage of users are still using the QEMU option. We’ve maintained QEMU support for backward compatibility, but both Docker VMM and Apple Virtualization Framework now offer:

  • Significantly better performance
  • Enhanced compatibility with macOS updates
  • Better integration with Apple Silicon architecture

If you’re currently using QEMU as your Virtual Machine Manager (VMM) on Docker Desktop for Mac:

  • Your current installation will continue to work normally during the 90-day transition period
  • After July 1, 2025, Docker Desktop releases will automatically migrate your environment to Apple Virtualization Framework
  • You’ll experience improved performance and stability with the newer virtualization options

The migration process will be smooth and straightforward:

  1. Users on the latest Docker Desktop release will be automatically migrated to Apple Virtualization Framework after the 90-day period
  2. During the transition period, you can manually switch to either Docker VMM (our fastest option for Apple Silicon Macs) or Apple Virtualization Framework through Settings > General > Virtual Machine Options
  3. For 30 days after the deprecation date, the QEMU option will remain available in settings for users who encounter migration issues
  4. After this extended period, the QEMU option will be fully removed

This deprecation does not affect QEMU’s role in emulating non-native architectures for multi-platform builds.

We recommend proactively switching to one of our newer VMM options before the automatic migration:

  1. Open Docker Desktop Settings > General
  2. Under “Choose Virtual Machine Manager (VMM)” select either:
    • Docker VMM (BETA) – Our fastest option for Apple Silicon Macs
    • Apple Virtualization Framework – A mature, high-performance alternative

If you have questions or encounter any issues during migration, please:

We’re committed to making this transition as seamless as possible while delivering the best development experience on macOS.

Keynote: CNCF at 10: Navigating Challenges, Embracing Opportunities - Joseph Sandoval, Principal Product Manager, Adobe; Liz Rice, Chief Open Source Officer, Isovalent at Cisco; Katie Gamanji, Senior Field Engineer, Apple

As the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) celebrates its 10th anniversary in 2025, we reflect on a decade of transformative growth in the cloud-native ecosystem which has reshaped how organizations build, deploy, and scale applications. From Kubernetes’ rise to the explosion of the cloud native ecosystem and landscape, CNCF has played a pivotal role in fostering open-source collaboration. However, this journey has not been without challenges—balancing innovation with stability, addressing security concerns in an era of increasing and emerging threats, and navigating the complexity of a rapidly evolving technology landscape.
In this keynote, we’ll explore how CNCF has tackled these challenges over the last decade and the lessons learned. We’ll also focus on the next decade: How will CNCF adapt to the changing tech landscape? What steps are being taken to simplify cloud-native adoption for developers and enterprises? And how can the community work together to ensure sustainability, security, and scalability in a cloud-driven world?</article>

Sponsored Keynote: Redefining MLOps at Scale on Top of Kubernetes - Randy Bias, Vice President of Open Source Strategy & Technology, Mirantis

In the mad scramble to get AI solutions up and running, we have ignored the existing challenges around Kubernetes sprawl and complexity. We are managing hundreds of Kubernetes clusters running thousands of cloud-native apps then … AI happened.

How do we address problems of scale without slowing down? Solving the sprawl means organizing Kubernetes into larger abstractions like those proposed in the Platform Engineering world, providing developers choice, while reducing toil, and codifying those de facto “Golden Paths” we all know so well.

What could an easier to use, faster to deploy, and more scalable Kubernetes-based AI internal developer platform (AI-IDP) look like for Platform Engineers? We will show a demo of deploying MLOps at scale with less pain and more joy.</article>

The explosion of data and the rise of AI agents have brought cloud-native systems to a critical tipping point. While the CNCF has driven significant advancements, the challenges of fault tolerance and state management in the age of AI remain unresolved both at the core and the edge.

This keynote examines how data gravity, distributed environments, and the complexity of AI workloads demand a fundamental shift from stateless architectures to intelligent, state-aware platforms. This provides a unique opportunity for the CNCF and its ecosystem to pave the way for a new generation of cloud-native, resilient, cost efficient, scalable, and AI-ready systems, while tackling the pressing challenges that lie ahead.</article>

Keynote: Adventures of Building a Platform as a Service for the Government - Hans Kristian Flaatten, Lead Platform Engineer, NAV & Audun Fauchald Strand, Principal Software Engineer, NAV

Who said that Government Tech has to be boring? In Norway the largest administration has been using Kubernetes for over 7 years! StatefulSets had just been introduced (alpha) and RBAC was still in beta. During this time we moved from quarterly releases to thousands of continuous releases each week across our fleet of cloud native applications!

Could we replicate the success we had at NAV for other agencies? Could we provide them with a fully managed platform as a service to let them focus on building new and innovative services for their users and not reinventing the wheel by building yet another platform?

In this session Audun and Hans Kristian will share their experience building and operating one of the largest platforms of its kind in Norway providing a fully fledged application development platform for more than a 100 product teams. And how they set an ambitious goal of being able to provide their platform as a service to other agencies.</article>

Sponsored Keynote: Hand Meet Glove: Why Kubernetes Will Become the Platform of Choice for Agentic Architectures - Idit Levine, Founder & CEO, Solo.io & Keith Babo, VP of Product, Solo.io

While we may not have a firm date on when AGI will emerge, one thing for certain is that 2025 will be the year of AI agents. Kubernetes is already the go-to platform for predictive analytics, MLOps, and inferencing today. What new challenges and opportunities emerge when autonomous, non-deterministic agents are deployed within our Kubernetes environments?

This talk will cover the primary building blocks of agents and multi-agent architectures and map them to Kubernetes primitives, patterns, and relevant ecosystem projects. We will discuss key challenges (and their solutions) in agentic architectures including:

    Adapting existing workloads and resources to integrate with agents via tool calling
    Observability and auditing to debug, diagnose, and explain agent behavior
    Sandboxing security context for agents to enforce principle of least privilege for the agent’s domain
    Connecting and orchestrating multi-agent interactions to support sophisticated workflows

items.title: Navigating the Inevitable: Kubernetes Breaking Changes Behind the Scenes - Marko Mudrinić
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Don't miss out! Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Navigating the Inevitable: Kubernetes Breaking Changes Behind the Scenes - Marko Mudrinić, Kubermatic GmbH

You're looking forward to a new feature, waiting for the release day like it’s Christmas morning. Suddenly, the feature is dropped from the release. Even worse, a feature you heavily depend on is unexpectedly deprecated or removed. What now? Negative emotions take over, you feel sad, frustrated, and even angry at the project and its maintainers. Fortunately, this doesn't happen too often. But it does happen.

The Kubernetes maintainers strive to make users satisfied, but they also have to prioritize the health of the project and the well-being of the maintainers. To do that, they sometimes have to make breaking changes, even on short notice, as hard as it might be. In this talk, we'll dive into some of those decisions, see what went on behind the scenes, and talk a bit about Kubernetes policies. Finally, we'll explore _your_ options as an end user, how you can be better informed, how you can provide feedback on proposed changes, and how you can help the project!</article>

Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Observability Pipeline Query Languages: Present and the Future - Jacek Migdal, Quesma

Many observability products have created their query languages, starting with Splunk and followed by a parade of incompatible options (Sumo Logic, Coralogix Dataprime, Grafana LogQL, Elastic ES/QL, OpenSearch PPL, to name a few). I’ll admit I’m one of the culprits who contributed to this fragmented landscape. Even PromQL, a well-known open-source option for time-series data, hasn’t reached the universal adoption levels of good old SQL.

Is there a way to untangle this mess and march toward some standardization? In this piece, I’ll dive into a few proposals, including concepts like “pipe SQL” and ideas floating around in CNCF forums, to see if there’s a glimmer of hope for alignment.</article>

Kubernetes SIG Storage: Intro & Deep Dive - Xing Yang, VMware by Broadcom & Jan Šafránek, Red Hat

Kubernetes SIG Storage is responsible for ensuring that different types of file and block storage are available wherever a container is scheduled, storage capacity management (container ephemeral storage usage, volume resizing, etc.), influencing scheduling of containers based on storage (data gravity, availability, etc.), and generic operations on storage (snapshotting, etc.). SIG Storage also has a project that provides APIs for object storage support in Kubernetes. In this session, we will deep dive into some projects that SIG Storage is currently working on, provide an update on the current status, and discuss what might be coming in the future.</article>

Join us at our next Flagship Conference: KubeCon + CloudNativeCon events in Hong Kong, China (June 10-11); Tokyo, Japan (June 16-17); Hyderabad, India (August 6-7); Atlanta, US (November 10-13). Connect with our current graduated, incubating, and sandbox projects as the community gathers to further the education and advancement of cloud native computing. Learn more at https://kubecon.io

Expanding the Contributor Pipeline Through Inclusion - Khallai Taylor, E.ON Digital Technology; Sandeep Kanabar, Gen; Stéphane Este-Gracias, ITQ; Nancy Chauhan, Independent; Josh Berkus, Red Hat

The growing demand for maintainers and contributors to sustain and evolve projects is an ongoing challenge. Yet, an untapped talent source—individuals from underrepresented groups—is often overlooked. Systemic barriers and a lack of inclusive practices most project members may not even realize.

This panel brings together members of TAG Contributor Strategy initiatives, including BIPOC, Women in Cloud Native, Blind and Visually Impaired, and Deaf and Hard of Hearing, to discuss how fostering inclusivity can unlock this potential. Panelists will share firsthand experiences of the barriers they’ve faced and discuss what a welcoming environment looks like from their perspectives.

Attendees will leave with actionable strategies to create more inclusive project spaces that attract and retain underrepresented contributors, ultimately ensuring the sustainability and vibrancy of open source for years to come.</article>

items.title: High Avalaible Monolith Posgresql Cluster with Patroni&Consul&Haproxy
items.author: Kaan Berk ÖZBEK
items.content:
High Avalaible Monolith Postgresql Cluster with Patroni&Consul&Haproxy

  • The runs on db1–db3 with Patroni and Consul, supporting automatic failover.
  • The is handled by HAProxy + PgBouncer on proxy1 and proxy2.
  • is provided by the virtual IP 10.0.0.30, always directed to the active proxy.
  • The is hosted only on proxy1.

The main goal of this setup is to eliminate single points of failure and ensure the PostgreSQL database service is continuously accessible. Each component in the architecture has a backup, and the system remains operational even if any part fails.

The following components and roles are used:

  • PostgreSQL + Patroni (3 nodes): These are the database nodes. Patroni manages the PostgreSQL cluster, determines the leader (primary) node, and handles automatic failover in case of failure. Patroni uses Consul as a distributed configuration store (DCS).
  • Consul (3 nodes, co-located with PostgreSQL nodes): Acts as the distributed key-value store required by Patroni for high availability. It maintains cluster node status and the leadership lock. A 3-node Consul cluster provides consistency using quorum-based decision-making (odd number rule).
  • : Serves as the routing and load-balancing layer. All application requests pass through HAProxy. It tracks the current primary (writable) node in the cluster and always forwards clients to the active leader. Optionally, it can distribute read loads across replica nodes.
  • : Acts as a database connection pooler. It reduces the load on PostgreSQL by pooling numerous short-lived connections from applications. When used with HAProxy, it provides both accurate routing and connection pooling. For high availability, PgBouncer runs on both nodes and is accessed via HAProxy.
  • Keepalived (2 nodes, alongside HAProxy): Provides a single access point by assigning a virtual IP (VIP) between two HAProxy+PgBouncer servers. The active HAProxy server holds the VIP; if it fails, the VIP automatically migrates to the other HAProxy server. This ensures clients always connect through the same IP, offering uninterrupted service.
  • pgAdmin (1 node, installed on one of the HAProxy/PgBouncer nodes): A web-based interface for managing and monitoring the PostgreSQL cluster. While not critical, pgAdmin is a convenience tool for DBAs/DevOps. In this setup, pgAdmin is installed on one of the HAProxy nodes (preferably the one that usually holds the VIP).

: In this architecture, the terms and will be used. The Primary node is the only writable database node; Replicas are read-only nodes kept up to date via streaming replication from the Primary. Patroni automatically selects and manages the Primary node. Client connections are always routed to the Primary; if the Primary changes, routing is dynamically updated thanks to the collaboration of Patroni, Consul, and HAProxy.

Servers and Sample IP Allocation

As an example, we will use the following IP address plan. You can adapt it according to your own network configuration:

  • (PostgreSQL + Patroni + Consul) — Hostname: db1, IP: 10.0.0.11
  • (PostgreSQL + Patroni + Consul) — Hostname: db2, IP: 10.0.0.12
  • (PostgreSQL + Patroni + Consul) — Hostname: db3, IP: 10.0.0.13
  • (HAProxy + PgBouncer + Keepalived + pgAdmin) — Hostname: proxy1, IP: 10.0.0.21
  • (HAProxy + PgBouncer + Keepalived) — Hostname: proxy2, IP: 10.0.0.22
  • (Keepalived Virtual IP) — 10.0.0.30 (shared virtual address for HAProxy/PgBouncer)

It is important that all servers have synchronized system time (via NTP) and correctly configured name resolution (via DNS or /etc/hosts entries).

For example, each server’s hosts file can include the others as follows:

10.0.0.11   db110.0.0.12   db210.0.0.21   proxy110.0.0.30   pgcluster-vip

Consul Installation and Configuration (DB1, DB2, DB3)

First, we will install the Consul distributed lock store that will be used by Patroni on all three PostgreSQL nodes. Since Consul is critical for consistent leader election among Patroni nodes, we will set up a Consul cluster running in server mode on each of the DB servers.

  1. Download and Install Consul:It is recommended to install the latest version of Consul using HashiCorp’s official repository.

    On each DB1, DB2, and DB3 server, follow these steps:

# HashiCorp GPG keysudo apt update
sudo apt install -y curl gnupg lsb-release
curl -fsSL https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg
# Add the HashiCorp repository to the APT sources list
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
    https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | \
    sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.listsudo apt update
sudo apt install -y consul

The steps above will install the consul package on the system. After the installation, a user will be created for Consul, and the default configuration directory will be located at /etc/consul.d.

:On each DB server, we will create a configuration file for Consul to run in server mode. Create the file /etc/consul.d/consul.hcl and configure it as shown below, using values specific to each server.

Example for DB1 /etc/consul.d/consul.hcl:

datacenter  = "dc1"node_name   = "db1"
bind_addr   = "10.0.0.11"         # The IP address of this server
client_addr = "0.0.0.0"           # Allow Consul UI and agent to listen on all interfaces
data_dir    = "/var/lib/consul"   # Data directory
log_level   = "INFO"bootstrap_expect= 3               # Total number of Consul servers in the cluster
retry_join      = ["10.0.0.11", "10.0.0.12", "10.0.0.13"]  # IP addresses of the other Consul servers
# (Optional) Enable the web UI
ui_config {}

Create the same file on and using their respective node_name and bind_addr values (db2/10.0.0.12, db3/10.0.0.13). Make sure that all are included in the retry_join list (the list can be the same on all nodes). With these settings:

  • Consul will run in on each node (with leader election).
  • bootstrap_expect = 3 indicates that the cluster will form quorum with 3 servers.
  • retry_join ensures that each node knows how to reach the others and form the cluster.
  • client_addr = 0.0.0.0 allows the Consul agent API to be accessible from all interfaces (for better security, you can restrict it to your internal network).

3. Start the Consul service and check the status:Once the configuration is complete, enable and start the Consul service on each DB server:

sudo systemctl enable consulsudo systemctl start consul

During the initial startup, you can view the cluster membership messages in the Consul service logs (or via journalctl -u consul). After all three servers discover each other, one will be elected as the . You can check the cluster status from any node using the following command:

This command will list all Consul nodes, show whether they are , and indicate their (leader/follower).If all members appear as , the Consul cluster has been successfully established.

Consul now provides a central service registry and distributed locking mechanism for Patroni.

:If you enabled the Consul web UI, you can also view the cluster status by navigating to http://<dbX-ip>:8500 in a browser.

(Access should ideally be done via a management network or a secure tunnel.)

PostgreSQL and Patroni Installation (DB1, DB2, DB3)

Now we will install on all three database servers.Patroni is a Python application that controls PostgreSQL processes and manages the cluster by communicating with other Patroni instances via Consul. Patroni takes over PostgreSQL’s configuration, startup, and shutdown processes. Therefore, we will run Patroni as the main service, not the PostgreSQL service itself.

Installing PostgreSQL (Ubuntu 22.04)

Install the PostgreSQL server package on each of the DB1, DB2, and DB3 servers:

sudo apt install -y postgresql postgresql-contrib

After the installation, Ubuntu will automatically start a default PostgreSQL cluster (14/main). Since we will manage our own cluster using Patroni, we need to stop and disable the default PostgreSQL service:

sudo systemctl stop postgresqlsudo systemctl disable postgresql

If necessary, you can also delete the default PostgreSQL data directory(usually located at /var/lib/postgresql/14/main).When Patroni starts for the first time, it will initialize its own data directory.

:Patroni must be able to find PostgreSQL binaries (like initdb, pg_ctl, etc.) in the system PATH. On Ubuntu, these are located under /usr/lib/postgresql/14/bin/. One way to make them accessible is to symlink them into /usr/bin:

sudo ln -s /usr/lib/postgresql/14/bin/* /usr/bin/

This allows Patroni to locate the required executables at runtime. Alternatively, you can set the PATH explicitly in the Patroni (as will be shown below).

Patroni is a Python-based tool and can be installed using pip. We will install Patroni with . On each DB1, DB2, and DB3 node, follow the steps below:

sudo apt install -y python3-pip python3-dev libpq-dev python3-psycopg2
sudo pip3 install patroni[consul]

This command will install Patroni and the required Python module (python-consul) to enable integration with Consul.

If this command fails with a psycopg2 error, it might mean the APT-installed psycopg2 didn’t take effect. In that case, you can also run:

sudo pip3 install psycopg2-binary

Verify that the installation was successful:

If this command returns the help output, Patroni has been installed successfully.

Each node running Patroni must have a YAML configuration file (e.g., /etc/patroni.yml). This file will be mostly identical across all nodes, but values such as , , and a few others will be adjusted per node.

Creating Patroni Configuration File

Below is an example Patroni configuration file:

scope: pgcluster            # Cluster name (must be the same on all nodes)namespace: /service/        # Prefix to be used in Consul KV (default is "/service/")
name: db1                   # This node's name (must be different on each server)
  listen: 0.0.0.0:8008
  connect_address: 10.0.0.11:8008   # API address for this node, accessible from other nodes
  host: 127.0.0.1  # Settings used during initial cluster bootstrap    ttl: 30    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576   # (1 MB) Max lag allowed for failover
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true             # Use pg_rewind if data is behind
      use_slots: true                 # Use replication slots to prevent data loss        max_connections: 100        max_wal_senders: 10        hot_standby: "on"
        hot_standby_feedback: "on"
        # Additional PostgreSQL parameters can be defined here (applies to all nodes)    - encoding: UTF8
    - locale: tr_TR.UTF-8   # Sample locale, change as needed
    - data-checksums
  # Set initial passwords when the cluster is created:
  users:      password: "YourPostgresSuperUserPassword"        - createrole    repl:
      password: "YourReplicationPassword"
      options:  listen: 0.0.0.0:5432
  connect_address: 10.0.0.11:5432     # This node's PostgreSQL address, accessible from other nodes
  data_dir: /var/lib/postgresql/14/main
  config_dir: /etc/postgresql/14/main  # Optional: PG config path, managed by Patroni
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/14/bin  # PostgreSQL binary path (needed if not in PATH)    replication:      password: "YourReplicationPassword"      username: postgres
      password: "YourPostgresSuperUserPassword"

Let’s briefly explain the above file:

  • : A unique name for the cluster. It must be the same on all nodes. This name will also be used in keys within Consul (combined with the namespace).
  • : Determines under which path Patroni will store records in Consul. The default /service/ is sufficient. (Consul KV entries will be stored under /service/pgcluster/.)
  • : The name of this Patroni node. It must be unique per server (e.g., db1, db2, db3).
  • : Patroni provides a REST API on each node to report its status.
  • listen: The IP:port the API will listen on.
  • connect_address: The address other cluster members will use to reach this node. Use 0.0.0.0:8008 for listen on all servers, but connect_address must be each node’s own IP.
  • : Specifies the DCS (Distributed Configuration Store) connection Patroni will use. Since the Consul agent runs locally on port 8500 on each server, we specify host: 127.0.0.1 and port: 8500.

    (If you use Consul ACLs, you can add a token and other parameters here, but we keep it simple.)

  • : Contains settings applied only when the cluster is first created.

    After bootstrap, Patroni saves some of these settings in Consul and reuses them later.

  • : Settings stored in Consul for the entire cluster.
  • ttl: Time-to-live for the leadership lock (e.g., 30s).
  • loop_wait: Time between checks (e.g., 10s).
  • retry_timeout: Timeout when retrying failed operations.
  • maximum_lag_on_failover: Max WAL lag allowed before failover.
  • : Enables automatic sync via pg_rewind if a node is behind (e.g., if the old primary rejoins).
  • : Uses replication slots to prevent WAL loss if replicas fall behind.
  • : These are PostgreSQL’s postgresql.conf parameters and will be applied on all nodes.

    Some important ones shown:

  • wal_level: replica, max_wal_senders, and hot_standby are essential for replication.
  • : If the data_dir is empty, Patroni will run initdb using these flags.

    You can define encoding, locale, and enable data-checksums (useful for detecting corruption, optional).

  • : Defines the PostgreSQL users and their passwords during the initial cluster bootstrap.

    We define a superuser (postgres) and a replication user (repl) with appropriate roles.

    These users are created automatically when initdb is run.
  • : Defines how Patroni will manage the PostgreSQL instance.
  • : The IP:port PostgreSQL will listen on. Usually 0.0.0.0:5432 to allow both local and network access.
  • : The address and port other nodes (and clients like HAProxy) will use to connect to this PostgreSQL instance.

    This should be the node's actual IP (e.g., 10.0.0.11:5432 for db1).

  • : The data directory for PostgreSQL. For APT installations, this is typically /var/lib/postgresql/14/main.

    This directory might already be initialized by APT; you may clear it manually.

    Patroni will run initdb on the first node and use pg_basebackup on the others.
  • : Directory for PostgreSQL config files, usually /etc/postgresql/14/main.

    Patroni will manage postgresql.conf, pg_hba.conf, etc. in this location.

    (Optional but recommended for clarity.)
  • : Path to PostgreSQL binaries.

    If you haven't added them to PATH, this ensures Patroni can find pg_ctl, initdb, etc.

    For PostgreSQL 14 on Ubuntu 22.04, this is /usr/lib/postgresql/14/bin.
  • : Credentials for Patroni to connect to PostgreSQL as the superuser and replication user.

    These should match the credentials defined in bootstrap.users.

    Patroni uses these to set up replication and perform superuser operations.
  • Create this configuration file on all three servers.
  • Adjust the following per server:
  • postgresql.connect_address
  • All other parts (scope, namespace, etc.) should remain identical across nodes.

: In this example, passwords are stored in plain text in the config file.

In production, you can use environment variables or .pgpass for storing credentials securely.

Make sure to restrict file permissions with:

chmod 600 /etc/patroni.yml

Let me know if you’d like the adapted config for db2 and db3, or if we should move on to setting up the systemd unit file for Patroni.

Systemd service description for Patroni

iniCopyEditDescription=PostgreSQL HA cluster node PatroniWants=network-online.targetType=simpleGroup=postgres
Environment="PATH=/usr/lib/postgresql/14/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni.RML
ExecReload=/bin/Kill -s HUP $MAINPID
KillMode=processLimitNOFILE=1048576WantedBy=multi-user.target
--sudo systemctl enable patroni
sudo systemctl start patroni

HAProxy setup and configuration (proxy1, proxy2)

HAProxy will route to the correct node in a cluster, client connections that now we will build layer. We have two HAProxy instance (proxy1 and proxy2 on servers), these will share with keepalived virtual IP (see below). HAProxy, which is the primary node that provides a REST API using Node Patroni each of which is a replica of the links that will understand and will route accordingly.

sudo apt install-y haproxy

2. Create the HAProxy configuration: default config file /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg is on the way. This is the end of file (or an appropriate substitute) we will add the required definitions for PostgreSQL. Existing settings to the defaults, dropping just at the bottom we show the parts we need:

# Optional: HAProxy global/defaults or stats interface config could go above
# Frontend for PostgreSQL Write Traffic (Primary Node)
frontend pg_rw    mode tcp
    default_backend pg_primary
# Backend for PostgreSQL Primary Node
backend pg_primary    option httpchk OPTIONS /master
    http-check expect status 200
    server db1 10.0.0.11:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server db2 10.0.0.12:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server db3 10.0.0.13:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
# Frontend for PostgreSQL Read-Only Traffic (Replicas)
frontend pg_ro    mode tcp
    default_backend pg_replicas
# Backend for PostgreSQL Replica Nodes
backend pg_replicas    option httpchk OPTIONS /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    server db1 10.0.0.11:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server db2 10.0.0.12:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server db3 10.0.0.13:5432 check port 8008 inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
# Optional: HAProxy Statistics Interface (for monitoring)
# listen stats#     stats enable#     stats auth admin:yourpassword
  • pg_rw frontend (port 5000): This frontend listens on port 5000 for incoming database traffic. All PostgreSQL clients should connect to this port for operations. We use mode tcp because HAProxy doesn’t inspect or interpret the PostgreSQL protocol—it simply forwards raw TCP traffic. This frontend is connected to the pg_primary backend, which ensures clients always reach the current  node.
  • This backend contains all three PostgreSQL nodes (db1, db2, db3), but only the current leader (primary) is considered healthy. HAProxy will direct traffic only to the node that passes the health check. option httpchk OPTIONS /master and http-check expect status 200:These lines configure HAProxy to send an HTTP OPTIONS /master request to the REST API of each node (port 8008). Patroni responds with: 200 OK on the current , 503 Service Unavailable on all replicas. This ensures HAProxy routes write traffic only to the leader. inter 3s fall 3 rise 2: Health checks are made every . If a node , it is marked as . If it then passes , it is marked as again. This results in about of detection time before failover takes place. on-marked-down shutdown-sessions:When a node is marked down (e.g., an old primary is no longer available), this option ensures active client sessions are forcibly closed. Clients will then reconnect—automatically establishing new sessions with the new leader.
  • : Only one node (the current leader) will be active in this backend at any given time. This guarantees that write requests never reach replicas by mistake.
  • pg_ro frontend (port 5001): This optional frontend listens on port 5001 for traffic. It connects to the pg_replicas backend, which is intended to distribute read load across replicas. If your application cannot separate read and write queries, this port may go unused.
  • Similar to the primary backend but in reverse logic: Uses health checks to /replica on port 8008. Only nodes will return 200 OK. The current responds with 503, and is marked unhealthy in this context. If you have multiple replicas, all will be active and HAProxy will use balancing. Optional: You can define separate ports for and replicas if fine-grained traffic control is needed.
  • Unified vs. Split Access Ports:You can either: Connect all traffic (read and write) to  — HAProxy will send everything to the primary. Or, split access — write operations to , read operations to  — if your application supports this.
  • HAProxy Statistics Interface (optional): You can enable a web-based stats dashboard by adding: This provides a live view of backend status, connection counts, and failover behavior. : Always protect this page with a strong password.

Installation and configuration Pooler (proxy1, proxy2)

Pooler, lightweight connection pool for PostgreSQL server. The minimum number of client connections to the database connection by turning many of the efficiency of the system improves. Our clients work directly on the proxy server and one shall be pgbouncer senaryomiz both in Pooler. Pooler via HAProxy will reach the correct database in the background. In this way:

  • Client-side will be provided (pooler for PostgreSQL as opening and closing the application havuzladig he wouldn’t have to fork a new process each time).
  • With HAProxy be integrated to Pooler will always connect to the correct node; failover in the event of re-establishing open connections will continue.
  1. both on the proxy server:
sudo apt install-y Pooler

This process Pooler creates the user and the service, but Debian/Ubuntu on default Pooler does not start automatically.

2. The default settings enable: /etc/default/Pooler file editor open and start=0 with the line Start=1 do. We are thus enabled to begin service systemd. Also PGBOUNCER_USER=Pooler and PGBOUNCER_GROUP=Pooler as you can leave the (default).

3. Pooler edit the configuration: main configuration file is /etc/Pooler/Pooler.the download is on it’s way. Examples include the default file.

[databases]# Clients that will access the database definitions.
# Format: logical_db_name = host:port dbname=actual_db_name pool_size=...
# Suppose we have a database named "myapp" that the application will use.
myapp = host=127.0.0.1 port=5000 dbname=myapp pool_size=20
# Optional: read-only traffic routed to replicas
myapp_ro = host=127.0.0.1 port=5001 dbname=myapp pool_size=20listen_addr = 0.0.0.0
# Authentication and pool settings
auth_type = md5
auth_file = /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt
pool_mode = session
max_client_conn = 500Explanation:
  • PgBouncer listens on port 5432 for incoming client connections.
  • It forwards requests to local HAProxy frontends (5000 for primary, 5001 for replicas).
  • myapp_ro is optional and used if the application separates read vs write connections.
  • pool_mode = session allows each client a dedicated connection for the life of their session.

Create /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt with database credentials:

"myappuser" "md5c2fcec2d60fd538e6294a7312d6237b7"
echo -n 'Sifre123myappuser' | md5sum
sudo systemctl start pgbouncersudo systemctl enable pgbouncer
psql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432 -U myappuser myapp

Keepalived VIP Configuration (proxy1, proxy2)

Because it is our proxy server with two clients connected to them we need to figure out how. virtual IP address between two servers by using we’ll hand over. Thus, the applications will be bound to the same IP; the IP will belong to this active proxy in the background. Proxy IP address automatically active when the other falls, and the clients will pass to seamless communication will continue via the same IP.

sudo apt install -y keepalived

Configuration File (Master)

vrrp_script chk_services {    script "/bin/pidof haproxy && /bin/pidof pgbouncer"    weight 2    state MASTER
    interface ens160  # replace with your network interface name
    virtual_router_id 51    advert_int 1        auth_type PASS    }        10.0.0.30/24 dev ens160        chk_services}

Configuration File (Backup)

vrrp_script chk_services {    script "/bin/pidof haproxy && /bin/pidof pgbouncer"    weight 2    state BACKUP    virtual_router_id 51    advert_int 1        auth_type PASS    }        10.0.0.30/24 dev ens160        chk_services}
  • vrrp_instance defines the shared VIP configuration.
  • priority determines which node is active (higher wins).
  • chk_services ensures HAProxy and PgBouncer are healthy before assigning VIP.
  • auth_pass and virtual_router_id must match on both nodes.
sudo systemctl enable keepalivedsudo systemctl start keepalived

Check the VIP on the MASTER:

The VIP 10.0.0.30 should be bound on the active node only. .Test failover by stopping HAProxy or PgBouncer on the MASTER node and watching the VIP move to BACKUP.

sudo apt install -y curl ca-certificates gnupgcurl https://www.pgadmin.org/static/packages_pgadmin_org.pub | sudo apt-key add -
sudo sh -c 'echo "deb https://ftp.postgresql.org/pub/pgadmin/pgadmin4/apt/ubuntu jammy pgadmin4 main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pgadmin4.list'
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y pgadmin4-web

Configure: sudo /usr/pgadmin4/bin/setup-web.sh

  • Password: Your set password

You’ll be able to see and manage the PostgreSQL cluster from the UI.

Conclusion and final checks

With these steps, 3-node (1 Primary + 2 Replica) PostgreSQL cluster to be managed by Patroni we was established. Two HAProxy+Pooler with a single virtual server can be connected via IP to the cluster has become clients. In this architecture , each component of backup available , because even though a failure at any point, the system continues to stay on his feet in a completely automatic waymedium.com. To summarize:

  • if the primary database node is interrupted, Patroni and HAProxy will detect this and automatically within a few seconds directs all traffic to the primary replica makes new bindmedium.com​percona.com. The application side, only a short interruption (disconnect and reconnect) is.
  • Load balancing (read Scaling): optionally, the HAProxy configuration read requests (port 5001) we can distribute to replica. Thus, reporting, analytics, such as read-intensive operations can be done without fraying bind. Just goes to bind processes that require writing.
  • connection Pooler thanks to hundreds of client controlled even with as many links as this is reflected in PostgreSQL. Mikroservis especially in architecture, each service has its own database instead of managing a pool of connections makes this center Pooler.
