items.title: Monitoring WMSPanel Channel Viewership on Linux with Python, Prometheus, and Grafana
items.author: Burak Ceviz
items.content:
In this comprehensive guide, we’ll walk through how to monitor channel viewership statistics (connected users) and from the using Python, Prometheus, and Grafana. You’ll learn how to set up API access, pull metrics using Python, expose them in Prometheus format, and visualize them in Grafana.

Why Monitor WMSpan Panel with Prometheus and Grafana?

For CDN and streaming platforms, monitoring real-time channel viewership is crucial for:

  • Understanding which channels are popular,
  • Tracking connected users in real-time,
  • Managing bandwidth usage effectively,
  • Making informed operational and capacity planning decisions.

While WMSpan Panel provides an API for these metrics, Prometheus and Grafana allow you to store, analyze, and visualize this data effectively.

1. Setting Up WMSpan Panel API

To access data from WMSpan Panel, you need to enable API access and whitelist your server’s IP address.

Step 1.1: Log In to Your WMSpan Panel Account

  • Log in to your WMSpan Panel account. If you don’t have an account, follow the platform’s registration process.

Step 1.2: Enable API Access

  1. Navigate to in your WMSpan Panel dashboard.
  2. The platform will generate a and . Save these securely; you’ll use them in your Python script.

Step 1.3: Whitelist Your Server’s IP

WMSpan Panel requires you to whitelist the IP addresses that will access the API.

  1. Go to in your WMSpan Panel dashboard.
  2. Add the IP address of the server where you will run the Python script.
  3. You can find your server’s public IP address using this command

2. Writing the Python Script

We’ll create a Flask-based Python application that:

  • Fetches server and stream data from the WMSpan Panel API,
  • Formats the data into Prometheus-compatible metrics,
  • Exposes the metrics via a /metrics endpoint.

Here’s the complete script:

from flask import Flask, Responseimport requestsimport threading
# Global dictionary to store metrics
metrics = {}
# Fetch server names and IDs from WMSpan Panel
def fetch_server_names():    try:
        url = "https://api.wmspanel.com/v1/servers?client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&api_key=YOUR_API_KEY"
        response = requests.get(url)        if data.get("status") == "Ok":
            for server in data.get("servers", []):
                server_id = server.get("id")
                server_name = server.get("name")
                if server_id and server_name:
                    server_name_map[server_id] = server_name
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error fetching server names: {e}")
    return server_name_map
# Fetch metrics from WMSpan Panel
def fetch_metrics():    server_name_map = fetch_server_names()        try:            for server_id, server_name in server_name_map.items():
                url = f"https://api.wmspanel.com/v1/streams?client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&api_key=YOUR_API_KEY&server={server_id}&realtime_data=true"
                response = requests.get(url)
                if data.get("status") == "Ok":
                    for stream in data.get("streams", []):
                        stream_name = stream.get("name", "unknown_stream").replace(".", "_").replace("/", "_")
                        connected = stream.get("connected", 0)
                        bandwidth = stream.get("bandwidth", 0)
                        label_string = f'name="{stream_name}", server_name="{server_name}"'
                        metrics[f"stream_connected{{{label_string}}}"] = connected
                        metrics[f"stream_bandwidth{{{label_string}}}"] = bandwidth            print(f"Error fetching metrics: {e}")
        # Update metrics every 30 seconds (retention time  like prometheus)
        time.sleep(30)
# Flask route to expose metrics
@app.route('/metrics')    global metrics    for metric_name, metric_value in metrics.items():
        metrics_output += f"{metric_name} {metric_value}\n"
    return Response(metrics_output, mimetype='text/plain')
if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=fetch_metrics, daemon=True).start()
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=9101)
  1. Fetch Server and Stream Data:
  • fetch_server_names(): Retrieves server IDs and names from WMSpan Panel.
  • fetch_metrics(): Retrieves stream data, including connected users and bandwidth usage, for each server.

2. Prometheus-Compatible Metrics:

  • Metrics are formatted like this:
stream_connected{name="stream1", server_name="CDN_01"} 123stream_bandwidth{name="stream1", server_name="CDN_01"} 4567890

3. Data Retention and Update Frequency:

  • The script resets metrics every 30 seconds.
  • time.sleep(30) ensures metrics are updated every 30 seconds to match Prometheus scrape intervals.

4. Expose Metrics via Flask:

  • The /metrics endpoint exposes metrics in a plain-text format for Prometheus to scrape.

3. Running the Python Script as a Systemd Service

To ensure the script runs continuously, we’ll configure it as a systemd service.

Create a file at /etc/systemd/system/wmspan_metrics.service:

[Unit]Description=WMSpan Panel Metrics ExporterWorkingDirectory=/path/to/your/script
ExecStart=/path/to/your/virtualenv/bin/python /path/to/your/script.py
Restart=alwaysGroup=rootWantedBy=multi-user.target

Run the following commands to enable and start the service:

sudo systemctl daemon-reloadsudo systemctl enable wmspan_metrics
sudo systemctl start wmspan_metrics
sudo systemctl status wmspan_metrics

4. Configuring Prometheus

To scrape metrics from the Flask application, update the prometheus.yml file:

scrape_configs:  - job_name: 'wmspan_panel'      - targets: ['localhost:9101']

Restart Prometheus to apply the changes:

sudo systemctl restart prometheus
docker restart prometheus_ID
cd /ur/prometheus/dockercompose/path/docker-compose up -d

5. Visualizing Metrics in Grafana

  • Total Connected Users per Server:
sum by (server_name) (stream_connected)

Use these queries to create panels in Grafana, visualizing your WMSpan Panel metrics.

  1. Enabled WMSpan Panel API and whitelisted our server IP,
  2. Created a Python-based Flask application to fetch and expose metrics,
  3. Configured Prometheus to scrape these metrics,
  4. Visualized the data in Grafana.

With this setup, you can monitor real-time channel viewership and from WMSpan Panel in a professional and scalable way.

items.title: Monitoring Linux File Descriptor Limits with Prometheus and Node Exporter
items.author: Burak Ceviz
items.content:
In this document, you will learn how to monitor the file descriptor information obtained via the cat /proc/sys/fs/file-nr command by using Prometheus and Node Exporter, visualizing it as a time series.

1. Importance of File Descriptors and the file-nr File

In Linux systems, the /proc/sys/fs/file-nr file contains important information about the current state of file descriptor usage. The content of this file is interpreted as follows:

  • Shows the number of file descriptors currently allocated.
  • A historical parameter, usually zero in modern systems.
  • Indicates the maximum number of file descriptors allowed on the system.

Monitoring this data is crucial for understanding how your system is utilizing its file descriptor resources.

2. Monitoring File Descriptors with Prometheus and Node Exporter

Prometheus is a powerful open-source tool used for collecting and monitoring system metrics. Node Exporter allows Prometheus to gather various system metrics from Linux systems.

Node Exporter provides two key metrics related to file descriptors:

  • Shows the number of file descriptors currently allocated.
  • Shows the maximum number of file descriptors allowed.

You can monitor these metrics with Prometheus and visualize them using a tool like Grafana.

3. Visualizing Time Series with Grafana

By visualizing the data collected by Prometheus with Grafana, you can observe how the usage of file descriptors changes over time on your system. Use the following queries in Grafana to visualize these time series:

Allocated File Descriptors:

Maximum File Descriptor Limit:

These queries provide essential metrics for monitoring the file descriptor usage on your system.

items.title: What is Fluent Interface in Programming?
items.author: Sourojit Das
items.content:
simplify code readability by allowing method chaining, making interactions more intuitive.

This design pattern enables developers to write cleaner, more expressive code by structuring method calls sequentially. It is widely used in APIs, automation frameworks, and database query builders to improve maintainability and reduce complexity.

This article will explore fluent interfaces, their advantages and limitations, and their implementation in popular automation frameworks.

What is Fluent Interface?

A Fluent Interface is a design pattern in software development that enables method chaining to create more readable, intuitive code that flows like natural language

The Fluent Interface Design Pattern helps programmers write code that reads smoothly and naturally.

It allows methods to be chained together, creating clear and readable programming sequences that feel more like conversational language.

Key Characteristics of Fluent Interface

Some key characteristics of the Fluent Interface are :

  1. Method chaining allows multiple operations in a single, flowing statement.
  2. Each method typically returns the object (this or self) to enable continuous chaining.
  3. The API reads like a domain-specific language, enhancing code readability and expressiveness.
  4. It often uses a declarative style, particularly for object configuration or complex operations.

Importance of Fluent Interface in Programming

Fluent interfaces are important because they enhance code readability and maintainability.

By allowing developers to chain methods together, they create a more natural, language-like syntax that’s easier to understand.

  • Enhances adherence to coding standards and best practices, enabling developers to write code that flows naturally and is easier for both technical and non-technical stakeholders to understand.
  • Improves code maintainability by providing a more intuitive and self-explanatory syntax, shortening the learning curve for new team members and facilitating code reviews.
  • It encourages a declarative programming style, focusing on what must be done rather than how. This results in more expressive and concise code with fewer intermediate variables.
  • Provides a more discoverable API, allowing developers to explore various options and configurations without continually referring to documentation, increasing productivity.
  • Provides a domain-specific language feel, bridging the gap between human language and programming syntax, leading to more comprehensible and business-oriented code.

Here are the key reasons why fluent interface is important in programming:

Example of Fluent Interface in Programming

The below C# snippet underlines how the Fluent Interface allows for a more expressive and intuitive way of setting up an employee object

FluentEmployee employee = new FluentEmployee()
    .NameOfTheEmployee("John Doe")

Each method call flows naturally into the next, reading almost like a sentence describing the employee.

When to Use Fluent Interface

Developers should consider using a Fluent Interface when building complex APIs requiring multiple configuration steps or enhancing code readability and self-documentation.

It’s particularly effective for scenarios involving builder patterns, configuration management, and operations that benefit from a step-by-step, intuitive method chaining approach.

How to Implement Fluent Interface

Step 1: Define the natural language syntax

Implementing a Fluent Interface requires creating a design that supports method chaining, resulting in a simpler and more expressive codebase.

Developers should consider creating a natural, accessible method sequence that mimics conversational language.

For example, a Report Builder function in C# looks like this -

Step 2: Define Supporting Interfaces

ReportBuilder.Create()
    .SetTitle("Monthly Sales")

The next step is to create interfaces that enforce method chaining and ensure type safety. For the same example given above, this would look like:

Step 3: Implement the Fluent Class

public interface IReportBuilder
    IReportBuilder SetTitle(string title);
    IReportBuilder AddSection(string section);

Then create a class that returns itself (this) to support continuous method chaining:

public class ReportBuilder : IReportBuilder
    private Report report = new Report();
    public static IReportBuilder Create() => new ReportBuilder();
    public IReportBuilder SetTitle(string title)
    public IReportBuilder AddSection(string section)
        report.Sections.Add(section);

Using Fluent Interface in Different Programming Languages

This section describes fluent interface implementation in a variety of popular programming languages :

This example shows a Fluent Interface for creating a report in C#. Each method in the returns this, allowing method chaining. This results in a more natural, sentence-like organization in the code.

public class ReportBuilder
    private Report report = new Report();
    public ReportBuilder SetTitle(string title)
    public ReportBuilder AddSection(string section)
        report.Sections.Add(section);
Report report = new ReportBuilder()
    .SetTitle("Monthly Sales Report")
    .AddSection("Detailed Analysis")
Console.WriteLine($"Generated report: {report.Title}");
Console.WriteLine($"Sections: {string.Join(", ", report.Sections)}");
  • Generated report: Monthly Sales Report
  • Sections: Overview, Detailed Analysis

When to use Fluent Interfaces in C#:

  • For configuring complex objects with multiple optional settings
  • When building SQL-like queries or operations, similar to LINQ
  • For creating nested configuration structures
  • When dealing with more actions than simple property setters
  • To improve code readability and create a domain-specific language feel
  • To make APIs more intuitive and self-documenting

This example demonstrates a Fluent Interface for building and sending an email. Each method in the returns a reference to *this, allowing for method chaining.

class EmailBuilder {
    EmailBuilder& setFrom(const std::string& sender) {
    EmailBuilder& setTo(const std::string& recipient) {
    EmailBuilder& setSubject(const std::string& emailSubject) {
    EmailBuilder& setBody(const std::string& emailBody) {
        std::cout << "Sending email:
"
                  << "From: " << from << "
"
                  << "Subject: " << subject << "
"
                  << "Body: " << body << std::endl;
        .setFrom("sender@example.com")
        .setTo("recipient@example.com")
        .setSubject("Meeting Reminder")
        .setBody("Don't forget our meeting at 3 PM.")

Sending email:From: sender@example.com

To: recipient@example.com

Subject: Meeting Reminder

Body: Don’t forget our meeting at 3 PM.

When to use Fluent Interfaces in C++:

  • For complex object initialization, constructor overloading becomes cumbersome.
  • When using template meta-programming to generate compile-time DSLs.
  • When you want to use C++’s operator overloading to construct expressive APIs.
  • For creating query-like structures comparable to the Boost.Spirit library creates parsers.
  • When using the Builder pattern on objects with several optional parameters.

This example demonstrates a Fluent Interface for building SQL queries. Each method in the returns this, allowing for method chaining.

public class SQLQueryBuilder {
    private StringBuilder query = new StringBuilder();
    public SQLQueryBuilder select(String... columns) {
        query.append("SELECT ").append(String.join(", ", columns)).append(" ");
    public SQLQueryBuilder from(String table) {
        query.append("FROM ").append(table).append(" ");
    public SQLQueryBuilder where(String condition) {
        query.append("WHERE ").append(condition).append(" ");
    public SQLQueryBuilder orderBy(String... columns) {
        query.append("ORDER BY ").append(String.join(", ", columns)).append(" ");
        return query.toString().trim();
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String sql = new SQLQueryBuilder()

SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 18 ORDER BY name

When to use Fluent Interfaces in Java:

  • When using Java 8+ Stream API for data processing and manipulation.
  • This is used to create complicated things, mainly using the Builder pattern.
  • When developing domain-specific languages (DSLs) in Java.

Fluent Interface in JavaScript

This example demonstrates a Fluent Interface for building database queries. Each method in the QueryBuilder class returns this, allowing for method chaining.

class QueryBuilder {
        this.query.fields = fields;
        this.query.collection = collection;
        this.query.condition = condition;
        this.query.limit = count;
const query = new QueryBuilder()
    .select(['name', 'email'])
    .where({ age: { $gt: 18 } })
console.log(JSON.stringify(query, null, 2));
{
  "fields": ["name", "email"],
  "condition": { "age": { "$gt": 18 } },

When to use Fluent Interfaces in JavaScript:

  • When working with asynchronous actions and Promises, to create more readable chains of.then() calls.
  • In Node.js stream processing, method chaining can simplify sophisticated data transformations.
  • To create Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs) in JavaScript, particularly configuration objects for libraries or frameworks.
  • When developing jQuery-style libraries for DOM manipulation and event handling.
  • Create more expressive and readable test assertions using test frameworks such as Mocha or Jest.
  • For creating sophisticated object settings, especially in frontend frameworks like React and Vue.js.
  • Using functional programming libraries such as Lodash or Ramda to develop data transformation pipelines.

This example shows a Fluent Interface for creating and sending emails. Each method in the returns $this, enabling method chaining.

class EmailBuilder
    private $attachments = [];
    public function to($email)
    public function subject($subject)
        $this->subject = $subject;
    public function body($content)
    public function attach($file)
        $this->attachments[] = $file;
        echo "To: {$this->to}
";
        echo "Subject: {$this->subject}
";

Sending email:To: recipient@example.com

Subject: Meeting Reminder

Body: Don’t forget our meeting at 3 PM.

Attachments: agenda.pdf, presentation.pptx

When to use Fluent Interfaces in PHP:

  • When creating query builders for database interactions, like Laravel’s Eloquent ORM.
  • For building configuration objects in PHP frameworks such as Symfony and Laravel.
  • When using form builders or validators in PHP applications.
  • As an alternative to the Builder pattern, it is used to create complicated objects with many optional parameters.
  • PHP test frameworks, such as PHPUnit, to provide more legible assertions and test settings.
  • When using PHP templating engines to develop more expressive view aids.
  • For creating Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs) in PHP, particularly for configuration and business rule definitions.

Fluent Interface in Python

This example uses a Fluent Interface to process a picture. Each method in the ImageProcessor class returns self, allowing method chaining.

class ImageProcessor:
    def __init__(self, image):
    def resize(self, width, height):
        print(f"Resizing image to {width}x{height}")
    def rotate(self, degrees):
        print(f"Rotating image by {degrees} degrees")
    def apply_filter(self, filter_name):
        print(f"Applying {filter_name} filter")
    def save(self, filename):
        print(f"Saving image as {filename}")
ImageProcessor("photo.jpg") \
    .save("processed_photo.jpg")

Resizing image to 800×600Rotating image by 90 degreesSaving image as processed_photo.jpg

When to use Fluent Interfaces in Python:

  • When working with data processing pipelines, such as pandas DataFrame operations.
  • Python can be used to create Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs), particularly for configuration or job automation.
  • To develop more expressive and readable test fixtures or assertions using test frameworks such as pytest.
  • When using builder patterns for complicated object development, especially in Python libraries or frameworks.
  • To develop more intuitive APIs in Python web frameworks such as Flask or Django.
  • When dealing with context, managers to develop a set of setup and takedown procedures.
  • When using Python’s dynamic nature to build flexible, chainable method calls.

Common Fluent Interface Design Patterns

Common Fluent Interface Design Patterns include:

  1. : Each method returns the object itself, allowing multiple method calls to be chained together.
  2. : Used for constructing complex objects step-by-step, often with optional parameters.
  3. Domain-specific language (DSL) Creates APIs that read like natural language, making code more expressive and easier to understand.

Difference Between Fluent Interface and Builder Pattern in Java

While both patterns strive to increase code readability and management, the Fluent Interface focuses on chaining method calls for readability. In contrast, the Builder Pattern gives an organized approach to building complicated objects, frequently including fluent interface in its implementation.

  • Fluent Interface: Enables method chaining, creating a more natural and expressive syntax.
  • Builder Pattern: This pattern allows for the step-by-step construction of complicated things, particularly when working with a large number of optional parameters or configurations.
  • Fluent Interface: Emphasises offering a straightforward API that enables chaining methods to conduct a sequence of tasks comprehensibly.
  • The Builder Pattern focuses on separating an object’s construction from its representation, allowing the same construction method to produce alternative representations.
  • Fluent Interface: Implemented by returning the current object (this) from methods, enabling method chaining.

This example demonstrates a query builder where we build a SQL query by chaining methods like select, from, and where.

import java.util.Arrays;
    private List<String> fields;  // Fields to select
    private String table;         // Table to query from
    private String condition;     // Query condition
    // Method to specify fields to select
    public Query select(String... fields) {
        this.fields = Arrays.asList(fields);
        return this; // Return the current object for method chaining
    // Method to specify the table
    public Query from(String table) {
        return this; // Return the current object for method chaining
    // Method to specify the condition
    public Query where(String condition) {
        this.condition = condition;
        return this; // Return the current object for method chaining
    // Build the final SQL query as a string
        return "SELECT " + String.join(", ", fields) +
public class FluentInterfaceExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
                .select("name", "age")       // Specify fields
                .from("users")              // Specify table
                .where("age > 18")          // Specify condition
                .build();                   // Build the query
        // Output: SELECT name, age FROM users WHERE age > 18
  • Builder Pattern: This pattern involves creating a separate Builder class with methods to set the object’s properties and a build() method to construct the final object.

This example illustrates constructing a User object with multiple optional fields using the Builder Pattern.

This approach ensures immutability and simplifies object creation.

public class User {
    private final String firstName;  // Required field
    private final String lastName;   // Optional field
    private final int age;           // Optional field
    private final String email;      // Optional field
    // Private constructor to enforce object creation via Builder
    private User(Builder builder) {
        this.firstName = builder.firstName;
        this.lastName = builder.lastName;
        this.email = builder.email;
    // Static nested Builder class
    public static class Builder {
        private String firstName; // Required field
        private String lastName;  // Optional field
        private int age;          // Optional field
        private String email;     // Optional field
        // Builder method for firstName (required field)
        public Builder firstName(String firstName) {
            this.firstName = firstName;
            return this; // Return the Builder object for chaining
        // Builder method for lastName
        public Builder lastName(String lastName) {
            this.lastName = lastName;
        // Builder method for age
        public Builder age(int age) {
        // Builder method for email
        public Builder email(String email) {
        // Build method to construct the final User object
public class BuilderPatternExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        User user = new User.Builder()
                .firstName("John")     // Set required field
                .lastName("Doe")       // Set optional field
                .age(30)               // Set optional field
                .email("john@example.com") // Set optional field
                .build();              // Construct the User object
        System.out.println("User Created: " + user);
  • Fluent Interface: Suitable for instances where several method calls are chained to configure or execute actions, improving code readability and frequently used in APIs such as Java Streams and query builders.
  • Builder Pattern: Suitable for creating complicated things with many parameters, especially when some are optional. It is helpful in constructing immutable objects and handling complex object generation logic.
  • Fluent Interface: Often works with mutable objects, with each method changing the object’s state and returning the same instance.
  • Builder Pattern: Typically used for creating immutable things. The Builder class is changeable and can be used to set properties, but the object returned by the construct() method is typically immutable.
  • Fluent interfaces are generally easier to implement because they add methods that return the current instance.
  • Builder Pattern: This pattern is more complex because it requires a separate Builder class and additional methods for setting properties and constructing the object.

Summary of Key Differences between Fluent Interface and Builder Pattern

In short, the differences can be summarized in the table below -

Advantages of Using Fluent Interface

Fluent Interfaces make code more elegant and readable by enabling method chaining.

  • Improves code clarity by making it more readable and intuitive.
  • Encourages declarative programming, focusing on what rather than how.
  • Simplifies object creation and setting modifications.
  • Eases code maintenance with self-documenting APIs.
  • Aligns programming with business logic, making it more expressive.

Limitations of Using Fluent Interface

Here are some disadvantages and limitations of using Fluent Interface:

  • Disrupts encapsulation, making it harder to track object states and leading to unpredictable behavior.
  • Increases testing complexity, especially when mocking or stubbing chained methods.
  • It can result in larger, more complex classes, affecting long-term maintainability.
  • Obscures underlying complexity, making it difficult for engineers to understand the code.
  • Reduces flexibility in API design and complicates future revisions.

Best Practices for Fluent Interface in Programming

Some best practices to keep in mind when implementing Fluent Interface are :

  • To support seamless method chaining, ensure that methods return the current object (this).
  • Maintain readability by designing method names and sequences that mirror natural language for intuitive and expressive APIs.
  • Keep procedures consistent and predictable; avoid introducing unexpected state changes while chaining.
  • When dealing with optional or complex setups, use overloading or constructors to add flexibility.
  • To retain readability and avoid debugging issues, avoid using too long chains.

Some Advanced Fluent API Techniques

Here are some advanced Fluent API techniques:

  • Method Overloading for Flexibility: Provide overloaded methods that can handle various parameter types or combinations.
  • Conditional Chaining: Use conditions within the chain to dynamically change behavior.
  • Immutability in Chaining: Return new instances in each step to ensure the original object remains immutable.
  • Custom Return Types for Contextual Chaining: Return various types depending on the context to prevent erroneous operations.
  • Error Handling in Fluent API: Use validations or exceptions when chaining to provide better feedback.

Fluent interfaces significantly enhance code readability and expressiveness, making complex operations more intuitive and self-documenting.

When implementing Fluent interfaces, developers should balance efficiency and simplicity, use clear method names, and consider the potential influence on testing and encapsulation.

Fluent interfaces in software testing and development can result in more maintainable and error-free code, particularly for test assertions, configuration, and domain-specific language generation.

items.title: Understanding Prometheus Metrics: A Comprehensive Guide for SREs and DevOps Engineers
items.author: Ibrahim Cisse
items.content:
At its core, Prometheus is a time-series database optimized for storing and querying metrics data. Its pull-based architecture, where the Prometheus server scrapes metrics endpoints, provides a reliable and scalable approach to gathering system and application telemetry.

Every Prometheus metric follows a consistent structure:

<metric_name>{<label_1="value_1">,<label_2="value_2">, ..., <label_N="value_N">} <metric_value>

Let’s break down each component:

  • : Identifies what’s being measured, following naming conventions like namespace_subsystem_unit_suffix. Examples include http_requests_total or node_cpu_seconds_total.
  • : Key-value pairs that add dimensions to metrics, allowing for more granular analysis. For instance, node_cpu_seconds_total{cpu="0",mode="idle"} specifies metrics for CPU 0 in idle mode.
  • : The actual measurement, stored as a floating-point number.

When Prometheus scrapes a target, it attaches a Unix timestamp (seconds since January 1, 1970, UTC) to each collected metric. These timestamps are crucial as they:

  • Enable time-range queries
  • Allow for trend analysis over specific periods
  • Facilitate correlation between metrics across different systems
  • Support alerting based on time-windowed conditions

Prometheus offers four fundamental metric types, each serving different monitoring needs:

Counters are cumulative metrics that only increase (or reset to zero, e.g., during restarts). They track totals over time, such as:

  • Total number of requests processed
  • Number of errors encountered
http_requests_total{method="GET", endpoint="/api/users"} 1234

Best used for: Rate calculations, events that accumulate over time

Gauges represent values that can increase or decrease, providing a snapshot of a current state:

    node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes{instance="server01:9100"} 10240000000

    Best used for: Point-in-time measurements, values that fluctuate

    Histograms sample observations and count them in configurable buckets, also tracking sum and count:

      http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"} 10234http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.5"} 24210
      http_request_duration_seconds_sum 53423.21
      http_request_duration_seconds_count 21322

      Best used for: Measuring distributions, calculating percentiles server-side

      Summaries are similar to histograms but calculate quantiles client-side over a sliding time window:

      http_request_duration_seconds{quantile="0.5"} 0.052http_request_duration_seconds{quantile="0.9"} 0.17
      http_request_duration_seconds{quantile="0.99"} 0.45
      http_request_duration_seconds_sum 53423.21
      http_request_duration_seconds_count 21322

      Best used for: Accurate percentile calculations when you need specific quantiles

      Labels transform metrics from one-dimensional data points into multi-dimensional insights. They enable:

      1. : Query only metrics matching specific criteria
      http_requests_total{status="500"}  # Only 500 status codes

      : Group metrics across various dimensions

      sum(http_requests_total) by (endpoint)  # Total requests per endpoint

      : Break down a metric by different properties

      rate(http_requests_total{endpoint="/api/users"}[5m]) by (status)  # Request rate by status code

      Effective monitoring typically includes labels for:

      • : Which server/container/pod
      • : What service/application
      • : Production, staging, development
      • : Geographical or logical location
      • : For multi-tenant systems
      • : Software version or deployment ID

      In Prometheus, every unique combination of a metric name and a set of labels defines a time series. For example:

      • node_cpu_seconds_total{cpu="0", mode="idle", instance="server1"}
      • node_cpu_seconds_total{cpu="1", mode="idle", instance="server1"}
      • node_cpu_seconds_total{cpu="0", mode="system", instance="server1"}

      Each represents a distinct time series, with values collected at regular intervals (the scrape interval). Understanding this model is essential for designing effective metrics and queries.

      Metric Design Best Practices

      When designing metrics for applications, prefer using labels over creating multiple similar metrics. For example:

      requests_auth_totalrequests_user_totalrequests_cart_total
      requests_total{path="/auth"}requests_total{path="/user"}
      requests_total{path="/products"}
      requests_total{path="/cart"}
      requests_total{path="/orders"}

      The label-based approach offers several advantages:

      • Easier aggregation with functions like sum(requests_total)
      • Better query flexibility with filtering by path label
      • More efficient storage in Prometheus
      • Simplified dashboard creation

      These conventions can be used for clear and consistent metrics:

      1. Use snake_case for metric names
      2. Include units in the name (e.g., _bytes, _seconds, _total)
      3. Follow the pattern namespace_subsystem_name_unit
      4. Use plural for aggregatable metrics (e.g., requests not request)

      While labels are powerful, excessive cardinality can hurt performance:

      • Avoid using high-cardinality labels like user IDs, email addresses, or unbounded values
      • Limit the number of label combinations to under 10–100 per metric
      • Consider aggregating or bucketing high-cardinality data

      For instance, instead of:

      http_request_duration_seconds{user_id="123456"}  # Problematic - too many unique users
      http_request_duration_seconds{user_tier="premium"}  # Better - limited number of tiers

      Instrumentation Strategies

      Application Instrumentation

      When instrumenting your applications, focus on these key areas:

      • ate: Requests per second
      • rrors: Failed requests per second
      • uration: Request latency distribution

      for resources:

      • tilization: Percentage of time the resource is busy
      • aturation: How overloaded the resource is (queue depth)

        Instrumentation Libraries

        Most programming languages have client libraries for Prometheus:

        • Go: prometheus/client_golang
        • Python: prometheus_client
        • Java: prometheus/client_java
        • JavaScript/Node.js: prom-client

        These libraries handle the metric types, exposure via HTTP endpoints, and follow best practices automatically.

        Query Language: PromQL Basics

        Prometheus Query Language (PromQL) is a powerful tool for analyzing metrics data. Here are some key functions:

        : Calculate per-second rates from counters

        rate(http_requests_total[5m])  # Request rate over 5 minutes

        : Calculate counter increase over time

        increase(http_requests_total[1h])  # Requests in the last hour

        : Aggregate across dimensions

        sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (endpoint)  # Total request rate by endpoint
        1. : Calculate percentiles from histogram buckets
        histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))  # 95th percentile latency

        Effective alerting is as much about what not to alert on as what to alert on:

        Alert on symptoms, not causes

        • Alert on customer-impacting issues
        • Let dashboards show the underlying causes

        Use rate and trend-based alerts

        rate(errors[5m]) / rate(requests[5m]) > 0.01  # >1% error raterate(errors[5m]) > rate(errors[1h] offset 1h) * 1.5  # 50% increase in error rate
        • Implement severity levels
        • Use appropriate time windows to avoid flapping

        Metric Lifecycle Management

        As systems evolve, metric needs change too:

        1. : Use comments or metric metadata to mark metrics for removal
        2. : Keep old and new metrics during transitions
        3. : Maintain a catalog of key metrics and their meaning
        4. : Pre-compute complex queries for efficiency

        Setting Up a Comprehensive Monitoring Strategy

        A complete monitoring setup typically includes:

        1. : CPU, memory, disk, network
        2. : Requests, errors, latency, queue depth
        3. : User actions, conversions, revenue
        4. : Probes testing critical paths
        5. : For contextual troubleshooting
        6. : For request-level insights

        As your environment grows, consider these scaling strategies:

        1. : Separate Prometheus instances for different purposes
        2. : Aggregate metrics from multiple Prometheus servers
        3. : Long-term storage via remote write adapters
        4. : Multi-tenant, highly available Prometheus setups

        Prometheus offers a robust, scalable approach to metrics collection and analysis. By understanding its data model, metric types, and best practices, you can build observability systems that provide valuable insights into your applications and infrastructure.

        • Use the appropriate metric types for different measurement needs
        • Design metrics with meaningful labels for better querying
        • Follow naming conventions and avoid cardinality explosion
        • Build a comprehensive monitoring strategy covering infrastructure, application, and business metrics

        With these principles in mind, you’ll be well on your way to creating effective monitoring systems that help maintain reliable, performant applications.

        🚀 If you found this guide helpful, please Clap, share, and subscribe to my blog!

        Your support helps me create more in-depth tutorials like this.

        Have questions or feedback? Drop a comment below I willlove to hear from you! 😊

items.title: Eloquent Casts in Laravel: Simplify Data Transformation
items.author: Zest Infotech
items.content:
Laravel provides a powerful feature called that helps developers automatically transform attributes when interacting with the database. This feature ensures that data is always in the expected format, reducing manual conversions and improving code readability.

Eloquent Casts allow you to specify how an attribute should be cast when retrieving or storing it in the database. By defining casts in your model, Laravel handles the type conversion seamlessly.

For example, suppose you have a User model and want to ensure the is_admin field is always treated as a boolean. Instead of manually converting it each time, you can define a cast in the model:

class User extends Model{        'is_admin' => 'boolean',}

Now, whenever you retrieve a User from the database, is_admin will always be a true or false value.

Commonly Used Casts in Laravel

Laravel provides several built-in cast types, including:

  • : Converts values to true or false.
  • : Ensures the value is always an integer.
  • : Converts numeric values to float.
  • : Automatically decodes JSON stored in the database into a PHP array.
  • : Similar to array, but maintains it as a JSON object.
  • : Converts timestamps into Carbon instances.
  • : Encrypts and decrypts data automatically.
class Order extends Model{        'total_price' => 'float',        'created_at' => 'datetime',}

Sometimes, you may need a custom cast to handle specific transformations. Laravel allows you to define custom cast classes by implementing the CastsAttributes interface.

Example: Creating a JsonToObject custom cast.

use Illuminate\Contracts\Database\Eloquent\CastsAttributes;
class JsonToObject implements CastsAttributes{
    public function get($model, string $key, $value, array $attributes)
    {
        return json_decode($value);
    }
    public function set($model, string $key, $value, array $attributes)    {
        return json_encode($value);
    }

Then, apply the custom cast in your model:

class Product extends Model{        'specifications' => JsonToObject::class,}

Now, specifications will automatically be converted between JSON and an object when saving or retrieving data.

Using casts in Laravel provides multiple benefits:

  • Improves code readability: Reduces manual type conversions.
  • : Ensures data remains in the expected format.
  • : Automatically converts attributes without extra code.

Eloquent Casts make handling data in Laravel much more convenient. Whether using built-in casts or custom implementations, they provide a streamlined approach to data transformation.

items.title: RabbitMQ and Spring Boot Integration: A Practical Guide
items.author: Yousef Mohamed
items.content:
What are Message Brokers?

  • : A software component or middle-ware that facilitates the transmission of messages between different systems, applications, or services.
  • : Acts as an intermediary that decouples the producers (senders) from the consumers (receivers) of messages.
  • Asynchronous Communication: Enables asynchronous communication, allowing systems to communicate without the need for direct or real-time interaction.

Importance of Message Brokers

Message brokers enable loose coupling between systems or services, meaning that the sender and receiver do not need to be aware of each other’s internal workings. This improves flexibility and maintainability.

Message brokers allow for scaling of applications by handling high message throughput. They ensure that messages are queued and processed independently, enabling consumers to scale up or down based on demand.

  • Reliability and Fault Tolerance:

With features like message persistence, acknowledgments, and retries, message brokers ensure that messages are not lost, even during system failures or crashes. This guarantees message delivery and increases the overall reliability of the system.

  • Asynchronous Communication:

They facilitate asynchronous communication, allowing systems to continue working independently without waiting for responses. This improves system performance and responsiveness.

Message brokers can distribute messages evenly among multiple consumers, preventing any single consumer from becoming overloaded and ensuring efficient use of resources.

Message brokers offer flexible message routing mechanisms, enabling messages to be directed to specific queues or topics based on certain conditions, improving how messages are handled and processed.

  • Enhanced System Integration:

They allow integration of heterogeneous systems by providing a common communication channel, enabling systems built on different technologies to exchange messages seamlessly.

  • Event-Driven Architectures:

Message brokers are central to implementing event-driven architectures, where services react to events asynchronously. This leads to more responsive and scalable systems.

  • Improved Monitoring and Analytics:

By centralizing message traffic, brokers can provide valuable insights into message flow, bottlenecks, and system performance, aiding in better monitoring and system health analysis.

  • Support for Complex Messaging Patterns:

Message brokers support various messaging patterns, such as point-to-point, publish/subscribe, and request/reply, making them suitable for a wide range of use cases in distributed systems.

Message Brokers vs. Message Queues

While both and are fundamental components in messaging systems, they serve different roles and have distinct characteristics. Here’s a comparison:

A message broker is a that facilitates the transmission of messages between multiple systems, applications, or services. It acts as an intermediary to ensure asynchronous communication, decouples producers and consumers, and may handle message routing, transformation, and persistence.

It supports multiple messaging patterns such as , , , etc.

A is a type of used by message brokers to temporarily hold messages until they are consumed. It follows the communication model, where messages are placed in a queue and consumed by a single consumer (or multiple consumers in some cases).

Manages and routes messages between multiple producers and consumers. It can perform complex message routing, filtering, and transformation based on predefined rules.

Supports multiple message delivery patterns like , , etc.

Stores messages temporarily until a consumer retrieves them. The queue typically holds messages in FIFO (First In, First Out) order, ensuring that each message is processed once by a single consumer.

Routes messages to appropriate queues, topics, or consumers based on message attributes.

Can transform messages into different formats before sending them to consumers.

Multiple Communication Patterns:

Supports complex messaging patterns, such as publish/subscribe, fan-out, or request/reply.

Message Persistence & Acknowledgments:

Ensures message delivery, even in case of failures, with message persistence and delivery acknowledgments.

Primarily holds messages in a queue for asynchronous processing by a consumer.

Basic Point-to-Point Communication:

Follows the pattern, where a message is delivered to a single consumer for processing.

Ensures that messages are consumed in the order they were placed in the queue.

Feature middleware for managing message flow and routing. Data structure for temporarily storing messages. Supports multiple patterns (pub/sub, point-to-point, etc.). Primarily point-to-point. Can route and transform messages based on rules. Simple storage without complex routing.

Queue-Based (Point-to-Point)

  • Messages are sent to a queue and consumed by a single consumer.
  • Once processed, the message is removed from the queue.
  • Ensures reliable delivery with message persistence.
  • : RabbitMQ (queue mode), ActiveMQ.

Publish/Subscribe (Topic-Based)

  • Messages are published to a topic and delivered to multiple subscribers.
  • Enables real-time event distribution.
  • : Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ (Pub/Sub).
  • Support both queue-based and publish/subscribe models.
  • Offer flexibility for various communication patterns.
  • : RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ

Advantages of Message Brokers

  • Producers and consumers communicate indirectly through a broker, reducing dependencies.
  • Enables independent development, deployment, and scaling of services.

Asynchronous Communication

  • Messages are sent and received independently, allowing systems to continue processing without waiting for responses.
  • Improves performance and responsiveness.
  • Handles large volumes of messages efficiently.
  • Supports horizontal scaling by distributing workloads across multiple consumers.

Reliability & Fault Tolerance

  • Ensures messages are and if a consumer fails.
  • Supports acknowledgments and redeliver mechanisms to prevent message loss.
  • Distributes messages across multiple consumers, preventing bottlenecks.
  • Ensures efficient resource utilization.
  • Supports different routing patterns like , , and .
  • Messages can be filtered, transformed, and directed to the appropriate recipients.

Enhanced System Integration

  • Enables seamless communication between services built on different technologies.
  • Facilitates microservices, event-driven architectures, and hybrid cloud environments.

Improved Monitoring & Analytics

  • Tracks message flow, processing time, and errors for system health monitoring.
  • Provides logging and tracing capabilities for debugging.

Support for Event-Driven Architectures

  • Helps build reactive systems that respond to real-time events.
  • Reduces tight coupling between event producers and consumers.

Security & Access Control

  • Supports authentication, authorization, and encryption for secure communication.
  • Prevents unauthorized access to sensitive data.

Use Cases of Message Brokers

  • Microservices Communication — Enables decoupled communication between microservices.
  •  — Supports real-time event processing and notifications.
  • Asynchronous Task Processing — Offloads heavy tasks to background workers.
  •  — Handles continuous data ingestion and analytics.
  •  — Distributes workloads among multiple consumers.
  •  — Centralizes logs for debugging and system insights.
  •  — Sends alerts via SMS, email, or push notifications.
  • E-Commerce Order Processing — Manages orders, payments, and stock updates.
  •  — Streams banking transactions and detects fraud.
  •  — Manages data exchange between connected devices.

Popular Message Broker Technologies

Traditional Message Brokers (Queue-Based & Pub/Sub)

These brokers handle reliable message delivery, routing, and persistence.

  •  — Lightweight, supports queue-based and pub/sub models.
  •  — Enterprise-grade message broker, supports JMS.

Microservices, enterprise integration, task queues.

Distributed Log-Based Message Brokers

Designed for high-throughput event streaming and real-time processing.

  •  — Distributed event streaming platform for real-time data.

Event-driven architectures, real-time analytics, log aggregation.

Cloud-Native Message Brokers

Fully managed services for scalability and serverless messaging.

  •  — Reliable messaging with enterprise features.

Cloud applications, serverless architectures, SaaS platforms.

IoT & Lightweight Message Brokers

Optimized for low-latency and lightweight messaging in IoT and mobile systems.

  •  — Efficient protocol for IoT devices.

IoT communication, embedded systems, real-time notifications.

Best Practices for Message Brokers

Ensure Message Durability & Persistence

  • Enable to prevent data loss (e.g., durable queues in RabbitMQ, log retention in Kafka).
  • Use to confirm message receipt before deletion.

Optimize Message Routing & Exchange Patterns

  • Choose the right (direct, fan-out, topic-based).
  • Use dead-letter queues (DLQs) to handle undelivered messages.
  • Use by adding more consumers.
  • Optimize (Kafka) or to distribute workloads.

Minimize Message Size & Complexity

  • Avoid large payloads; use compressed or lightweight formats (e.g., JSON, Protocol Buffers).
  • Avoid unnecessary headers and metadata.

Implement Idempotency & Deduplication

  • Assign to prevent processing the same message multiple times.
  • Use (e.g., Kafka exactly-once semantics) when needed.

Secure Messaging Channels

  • Enable for message transport.
  • Implement authentication & authorization (e.g., OAuth, JWT, API keys).

Monitor & Log Message Broker Performance

  • Use (Prometheus, Grafana, ELK) to track queue length, consumer lag, and failures.
  • Set up for high message backlog, unprocessed messages, or broker failures.

Use Retry & Backoff Strategies

  • Implement for failed message processing retries.
  • Use (Resilience4j, Hystrix) to prevent system overloads.

Manage Broker Resources Efficiently

  • Tune memory, disk, and network configurations for high throughput.
  • Use and efficient consumer batch processing.

Design for High Availability & Fault Tolerance

  • Deploy brokers in (e.g., Kafka replication, RabbitMQ HA queues).
  • Distribute brokers across multiple availability zones for disaster recovery.

Architecture and Implementation with RabbitMQ

RabbitMQ is a that facilitates communication between distributed systems using the AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol). It supports , ensuring reliable and scalable message delivery.

  1.  — Sends messages to RabbitMQ.
  2.  — Routes messages based on binding rules.
  3.  — Stores messages until consumed.
  4.  — Retrieves and processes messages.
  5.  — The RabbitMQ server managing messaging.

Message Routing in RabbitMQ:

  •  — Messages are sent to a queue with a specific routing key.
  •  — Messages are broadcasted to all bound queues.
  •  — Messages are routed based on pattern matching.
  •  — Routing based on message headers.

2. RabbitMQ Implementation

# Install RabbitMQ on Ubuntusudo apt update
sudo apt install rabbitmq-server -y
sudo systemctl enable rabbitmq-server
sudo systemctl start rabbitmq-server

Enable RabbitMQ Management UI (Optional):

sudo rabbitmq-plugins enable rabbitmq_management

Step 1.2: Configure RabbitMQ

Create a user & set permissions:

sudo rabbitmqctl add_user myuser mypasswordsudo rabbitmqctl set_user_tags myuser administrator
sudo rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p / myuser ".*" ".*" ".*"

Step 1.2: Image on Docker

You can quickly set up RabbitMQ using for easy deployment and testing.

Step 2.1: Pull the RabbitMQ Docker Image

Run the following command to download the RabbitMQ image with the management plugin (for UI access):

sudo docker pull rabbitmq:3-management

Step 2.2: Run RabbitMQ Container

sudo docker run -d --name rabbitmq \\  -p 5672:5672 -p 15672:15672 \\
  -e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_USER=myuser \\
  -e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_PASS=mypassword \\
  • d → Runs the container in detached mode.
  • -name rabbitmq → Names the container rabbitmq.
  • p 5672:5672 → Exposes RabbitMQ's AMQP port for messaging.
  • p 15672:15672 → Exposes the RabbitMQ Management UI.
  • e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_USER=username → Sets the default username.
  • e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_PASS=password → Sets the default password.

Step 2.3: Verify RabbitMQ is Running

    sudo docker logs rabbitmq

    Step 2: Implement RabbitMQ in Java (Spring Boot)

    <dependency>    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-amqp</artifactId>
    </dependency>

    Step 3: Configure RabbitMQ in Spring Boot

    spring:  rabbitmq:    port: 5672    password: mypassword  exchange:
        requests: exchange.requests
      queue:  route:
        requests: routing_key.requests
    spring.rabbitmq.host= localhostspring.rabbitmq.port= 5672
    spring.rabbitmq.username= myuser
    spring.rabbitmq.password= mypassword
    rabbitmq.exchange.requests= exchange.requests.
    rabbitmq:queue.requests= queue.requests
    rabbitmq:route.requests= routing_key.requests

    Step 4: Create a Producer (Message Sender)

    @Servicepublic class MessageProducer {
        private final RabbitTemplate rabbitTemplate;
        private static final Logger LOGGER = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MessageProducer.class);
        MessageProducer(RabbitTemplate rabbitTemplate) {
            this.rabbitTemplate = rabbitTemplate;
        }
        @Value("${rabbitmq.exchange.requests}")
        private String exchange;
        @Value("${rabbitmq.route.requests}")
        private String routingKey;
        public void send(String message) {
            LOGGER.info("send message: {}", message);
            rabbitTemplate.convertAndSend(exchange, routingKey, message);
        }

    Step 5: Create a Consumer (Message Receiver)

    create MessageConsumer.java

    @RequiredArgsConstructor@Service
    public class MessageConsumer {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MessageConsumer.class);@RabbitListener(queues = "${rabbitmq.queue.requests}")
        public void receiveMessage(String message) {
            log.info("receive message: {}", message);}

    Step 6: Define Queue, Exchange, and Binding

    • create RabbitmqConfig.java
    @Configurationpublic class RabbitmqConfig {    @Value("${rabbitmq.exchange.requests}")
        public String EXCHANGE_REQUESTS;
        @Value("${rabbitmq.queue.requests}")
        public String QUEUE_REQUESTS;
        @Value("${rabbitmq.route.requests}")
        public String ROUTING_KEY_REQUESTS;
        @Bean
        DirectExchange requestExchange(){
            return new DirectExchange(EXCHANGE_REQUESTS);    @Bean
        DirectExchange responseExchange(){
            return new DirectExchange(EXCHANGE_RESPONSES);    @Bean        return new Queue(QUEUE_REQUESTS,true);    Binding requestBinding(Queue requestQueue, DirectExchange requestExchange){
            return BindingBuilder.bind(requestQueue)
                    .to(requestExchange)
                    .with(ROUTING_KEY_REQUESTS);
        }
    • create rabbitmq service implementation RabbitService.java
    @RequiredArgsConstructor@Service
    public class RabbitmqService {
        private MessageProducer producer ;    public String create(String message) {       return message;}
    • create controller or adapter
    @RequiredArgsConstructor@RequestMapping("/api/rabbitnmq")
    public class RabbitmqController {
        private final RabbitmqService mRabbitService;
        public ResponseEntity<String> postMessage(@RequestBody String message) {
            return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(mRabbitService.create(message));
        }
    curl -X POST <http://localhost:8081/api/rabbitmq/create> \\     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \\
         -d '"This Message is an example to test RabbitMq"'

    🐇 RabbitMQ Demo with Spring Boot

    This project demonstrates how to integrate with a application. It includes:

    • : To send messages to RabbitMQ.
    • : To receive and process messages.
    • : To interact with products and trigger message publishing.
    • JsonMessageProducer: Sends product data as JSON messages.
    • MessageProducer: Sends simple string messages.
    • JsonMessageConsumer: Listens for JSON messages and converts them into Product objects.
    • MessageConsumer: Listens for simple text messages.

    REST API for Product Management

    • ProductController: Exposes endpoints to create and fetch products.
    • ProductService: Handles business logic and integrates with RabbitMQ.
    • RabbitmqConfig: Defines exchanges, queues, and routing keys.
    src/main/java/com/eagle0eye/rabbitmq_demo│── core│   │   ├── application
    │   │   │   ├── service/IProductService.java
    │   │   │   ├── impl/ProductService.java
    │   │   │   ├── mapper/ProductMapper.java
    │   │   ├── domain/Product.java
    │   │   ├── dto/ProductRequestDTO.java
    │   │   ├── dto/ProductResponseDTO.java│   ├── rabbitmq
    │   │   ├── config/RabbitmqConfig.java
    │   │   ├── consumer/JsonMessageConsumer.java
    │   │   ├── consumer/MessageConsumer.java
    │   │   ├── producer/JsonMessageProducer.java
    │   │   ├── producer/MessageProducer.java
    │── api
    │   ├── controller/ProductController.java

items.title: AI Assisted DevOps: Day 2 , Prompt Engineering
items.author: Kuseh Simon Wewoliamo
items.content:
AI Assisted DevOps: Prompt Engineering

What is Prompt Engineering?

In a nutshell, Prompt Engineering (PE) can be described as the technique of crafting natural language text to guide Generative AI to generate a desired output. Gen-AI tries to mimic humans, but it still requires very detailed instructions to produce high-quality and desired outputs. Prompt Engineering is an essential skill that everyone should have when it comes to Generative AI. The quality of your prompt (Input) to a Gen-AI determines your quality of your desired output.

Prompt Engineering workflow

To craft a good prompt , above is the workflow one can use as a guide:

  1. You should clearly identify what you want to achieve with Gen-AI. A well define objective or goal will help you to craft a good prompt.
  2. Collect some relevant information about your environment, requirements, and constraints that will help in crafting a good prompt.
  3. Create your first version of the prompt
  4. Submit/Execute your prompt to the Gen-AI system
  5. Assess whether the result meets your needs and can accomplish your objective. If the output is satisfactory, implement the solution ,document the prompt and build a prompt library.

For unsatisfactory outputs identify the problem which can be due to:

1. Lack of Context (Add Relevant Details)

2. Wrong Format (Specify Output Format)

3. Inaccurate Results (Add Constraints/Examples)

4. Too General (Add more Specificity)

update your prompt and execute again.

Why Prompt Engineering is important to DevOps Engineers?

Prompt engineering can help DevOps engineers some

✅. Generate optimized CI/CD scripts (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI).

✅. Automate Kubernetes, Terraform, and Ansible configurations.

✅. Assist in troubleshooting and debugging.

✅. Generate custom Bash, Python, or YAML scripts.

✅. Improve incident response and root cause analysis.

Prompt Engineering Techniques

Zero-shot prompting means that the prompt used to communicate with the model does not include any examples or demos. With zero-shot prompt, you instruct the model to perform a task without any examples to guide it.

Generate a Shell Script to Backup Logs

#!/bin/bash# Configuration
LOG_DIR="/var/log"  # Directory containing log files
BACKUP_DIR="/backup/logs"  # Backup storage location
MAX_BACKUPS=5  # Number of backups to retain
TIMESTAMP=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S")
BACKUP_FILE="$BACKUP_DIR/logs_backup_$TIMESTAMP.tar.gz"
# Create backup directory if it does not exist
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"
# Create a compressed backup of the logs
tar -czf "$BACKUP_FILE" -C "$LOG_DIR" .
# Remove old backups, keeping only the most recent $MAX_BACKUPS backups
ls -t "$BACKUP_DIR"/logs_backup_*.tar.gz | tail -n +$((MAX_BACKUPS + 1)) | xargs rm -f
# Print completion message
echo "Backup completed: $BACKUP_FILE"

In few-shot prompting, you provide the model with some few examples, usually 2–3 examples to help guide the AI in its response.

Here are some shell scripts to manage logs:

echo “Application started” > /var/logs/app.log

tar -czf /backup/app_logs.tar.gz /var/logs/*

Now write a script to delete logs older than 7 days.

#!/bin/bashLOG_DIR="/var/logs"
# Find and delete log files older than 7 days
find $LOG_DIR -name "*.log" -type f -mtime +7 -exec rm {} \;
echo "Logs older than 7 days have been deleted."
# Optionally log this cleanup operation
echo "$(date): Log cleanup performed - files older than 7 days removed" >> $LOG_DIR/cleanup.log

Multi-shot prompting is an extension of few-shot prompting where you provide multiple examples and progressively refine the task.

kubectl create deployment nginx — image=nginx

kubectl get deployment nginx

kubectl describe deployment nginx

Now write a command to scale the nginx deployment to 5 replicas.

kubectl scale deployment nginx --replicas=5

### 4 Chain of Thought (CoT) Prompting

chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting enables complex reasoning capabilities through intermediate reasoning steps. Here, you ask the AI to detail its thought process step-by-step. This is particularly useful for complex reasoning tasks.

My shell script is failing. Here is the script:

#!/bin/bashmkdir -p /backup

It shows a “permission denied” error. How can I fix it? Please explain step-by-step.

I’ll walk you through diagnosing and fixing the permission denied error step by step:

Permission Denied Causes:

The “permission denied” error typically occurs because:

The script is being run by a user without sufficient privileges

The destination or source directories have restricted permissions

Solution 1: Run with Sudo

Or prefix the commands with sudo:

 #!/bin/bashsudo mkdir -p /backup
sudo cp /var/logs/* /backup/

Solution 2: Check Current User and Permissions

  # Check current userwhoami
# Check permissions of source and destination directories
ls -l /var/logs

Solution 3: Modify Permissions

    # Give write permissions to the backup directorysudo chmod 777 /backup
# Or change owner of backup directory
sudo chown $(whoami):$(whoami) /backup

Solution 4: Use Specific User Permissions

    #!/bin/bash# Run as root or with specific user permissionssudo cp /var/logs/* /backup/

Recommended Debugging Steps:

Verify you have root or sudo access

Check directory permissions

Ensure source files are readable

Verify destination directory exists and is writable

Use Cases for DevOps Prompt Engineering

Here are some use cases that for AI in DevOps

✅ 1. Generating IaC (Infrastructure as Code)

✅ 2. CI/CD Pipeline Automation and Optimization

✅ 3. Technical Documentation

✅ 4. Generate custom Bash, Python, or YAML scripts

✅ 5. Log Analysis & Incident Response

Best Practices for Prompt Engineering

1️⃣. Be clear and specific — The more specific the prompt, the better the output.

2️⃣. Use context — Provide background information or examples when needed.

3️⃣. Define the Output Format- Always specify the format you need

4️⃣.Iterate and refine — If the output isn’t ideal, adjust the prompt.

5️⃣.Use CoT for complex tasks — Step-by-step reasoning improves accuracy.

With AI evolving, prompt engineering will play a crucial role in how DevOps engineers prompt LLMs for their day-to-day activities. Mastering effective prompting will make you a 10x DevOps engineer! 🚀. My Github repo contains details about the 10 Day AI Assisted DevOps.

items.title: Docker Container Creation Flow: A Simple Guide
items.author: Subbareddysangham
items.content:
Have you ever wondered what happens when you type a simple command? While it might seem like magic, there's a fascinating sequence of events that unfolds behind the scenes. In this blog post, I'll break down the Docker container creation process in simple terms that anyone can understand.

The 8-Step Journey of a Docker Container

Step 1: Docker Client Sends Command

When you type a command like in your terminal, you're talking to the Docker client. This client:

  • Sends it to the Docker daemon (the engine that does the actual work)

Think of it like ordering at a restaurant — you (the user) tell the waiter (Docker client) what you want, and they take your order to the kitchen (Docker daemon).

Step 2: Docker Daemon Checks Local Image

Once the Docker daemon receives your request, it first checks if it already has the image (in our example, nginx) on your computer.

If it doesn’t find the image:

  • It connects to Docker Hub (or another registry you’ve configured)
  • Stores it locally for future use

This is like the kitchen checking if they have all the ingredients. If not, they send someone to the store to get what’s needed.

Step 3: Create Container with OCI Runtime

The Docker daemon doesn’t actually create the container itself. Instead, it hands off this job to an OCI (Open Container Initiative) runtime, typically . This standardized container runtime:

  • Creates container specifications
  • Prepares everything for the Linux kernel

Step 4: Kernel Creates Namespaces & Cgroups

Now the Linux kernel steps in and creates two important things:

  • : These provide isolation by making the container think it has its own copy of resources (network interfaces, process IDs, etc.)
  • (Control Groups): These limit how many resources the container can use (CPU, memory, etc.)

Namespaces are like giving the container its own private room, while cgroups are like setting rules for how much of the house’s electricity and water it can use.

Step 5: Assign Virtual Network

Docker sets up networking so your container can communicate:

  • Creates a virtual network interface (veth pair)
  • Connects one end to the container and one end to the Docker bridge
  • Assigns the container an IP address
  • Sets up DNS resolution so the container can find other services by name

This is similar to setting up a new phone with its own phone number and contact list.

Step 6: Add Writable Layer

Docker images are made up of read-only layers, but containers need a place to write data. Docker:

  • Mounts all the read-only image layers
  • Adds a new writable layer on top where all changes will be stored
  • Uses a union filesystem to combine these layers so they appear as one filesystem

Think of this like giving someone a reference book (read-only) and a notebook (writable) — they can read from the book but write their notes in the notebook.

With everything set up, Docker now executes the container’s default command (usually defined in the image’s Dockerfile as CMD or ENTRYPOINT). This launches the main process that the container will run.

Step 8: Output Streamed Back

Finally, any output from the container (logs, error messages, etc.) is captured by Docker and streamed back to your terminal. This allows you to see what’s happening inside the container even though it’s running in isolation.

This is the power of Docker’s container creation flow — complex operations are abstracted away, letting you focus on your application rather than infrastructure details.

Client → Daemon → Image → Runtime → Kernel → Network → Filesystem → Execute → Output

Understanding the 8 steps of Docker’s container creation flow helps you better utilize this powerful technology. Next time you run a Docker container, you’ll have a much clearer picture of what’s happening under the hood.

From sending a simple command to having a fully functional, isolated application environment, Docker handles the complexity for you — but now you know how it works!

Follow for more practical guides and technical deep-dives into containerization and DevOps practices!

items.title: Integrating AI Systems with the Model Context Protocol (MCP)
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
Effectively integrating Artificial Intelligence (AI) agents with various data sources or external tools remains a constant challenge in the development of intelligent solutions. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) emerges as a solution to standardize this communication, enhancing interoperability and reusability of functionalities. This article outlines the key aspects of MCP, highlighting its purpose, operation, and benefits.

Below, we present the fundamental concepts you need to understand the MCP at a high level.

MCP is an open protocol designed to standardize how applications provide context to Large Language Models (LLMs). It enables AI agents to connect uniformly to various data sources and tools, eliminating the need for custom integrations for each agent or client.

2. The Problem MCP Solves

Without MCP, each AI agent requires specific code to integrate with external APIs, such as Slack, Gmail, or databases. This approach results in redundant implementations and increased system complexity. MCP addresses this issue by offering a standardized interface that enables reuse of already implemented logic.

The protocol follows a client-server architecture. MCP hosts represent the programs seeking to access data — such as AI applications. MCP clients maintain connections with MCP servers, which are lightweight and modular programs exposing specific capabilities through a standardized API. These servers can access local data sources, such as files or databases, and also connect to remote services over the internet.

This setup allows developers to create servers that expose capabilities like sending emails or retrieving weather forecasts. An AI agent only needs to be MCP-compatible to access any existing MCP server without requiring additional custom integrations.

The diagram below illustrates the difference between traditional direct integrations and the standardized approach enabled by MCP. On the left, each connection must be implemented individually. On the right, the MCP Server acts as an abstraction layer, simplifying communication and reducing development complexity.

4. Practical Example: Integration in a Pastry Shop

Imagine a delivery management system used by a fictional pastry shop to plan and monitor scheduled customer deliveries. To ensure operational efficiency, the system must:

  • Check the weather forecast for delivery locations.
  • Notify customers of potential delays due to adverse weather.
  • Automate decision-making using external data, such as weather APIs.

To support this use case, an AI-powered system is developed to automate operational decisions, improving agility and customer experience.

Two tools are made available through the MCP server to support the workflow:

  • get_forecast: queries the weather forecast based on latitude and longitude.
  • send_notification: sends messages to customers based on predefined conditions.

The AI agent performs the following steps:

  • Receives an order and identifies the need to check weather conditions.
  • Converts the address to geographic coordinates.
  • Queries the MCP server using the get_forecast function.
  • Analyzes the response and, if needed, uses send_notification to inform the customer about potential delays.

This flow enables efficient integration with minimal additional coding, supporting scalable implementation.

MCP offers several important advantages:

  • functionalities are implemented once and can be reused across multiple agents.
  • new tools become immediately accessible to MCP-compatible agents.
  • it is possible to restrict which functionalities are exposed to specific agents.
  • MCP servers can be built in any language and encapsulate any required logic.

Key use cases for MCP include:

  • Integration with services like Slack, Gmail, GitHub, and databases using custom wrappers or tools provided by frameworks such as LangChain.
  • Expansion of AI agents with new features via external MCP servers, without modifying the agent’s original code.
  • Development of modular pipelines enabling dynamic composition of functionalities according to each application’s needs.
  • Building multi-agent platforms where a single MCP backend supports multiple distinct contexts.

7. Security Considerations

Running tools via MCP may involve third-party code execution, which requires safeguards such as user review and approval or isolated environments to mitigate risks.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) offers a standardized approach to integrating AI agents with external systems. By reducing development complexity while enhancing reusability and scalability, MCP positions itself as a key component in modular and interoperable AI-driven architectures.

Videos and Courses on the Topic

Using Ansible Galaxy role to Deploy EC2 instances to configure Web Application, Nginx and setup Nginx to route the traffic to access the webpage

In this blog, I am going to use ansible to create EC2 instances, updated the hosts files with the required details, then I will deploy and setup to one of it, after this I will setup “” service to deploy a webpage which will be accessible with load balancer.

  • Ansible installed and configured in local system/cloud hosted system
  1. To deploy an web application, I need servers ready to configure them. Here, I will be using the Ansible Galaxy Role to deploy the EC2 instances and record their IP address to the default hosts file ().
  2. To Deploy the role, I moved to default location where ansible saves all the roles i.e., the path and executed the below command to create the role named .
ansible-galaxy role init ec2_instance

3. It will have the below folder structures.

[root@DESKTOP-SNBLIVS ec2_instance]# tree.├── defaults├── files│   └── main.yml│   └── main.yml│   └── main.yml├── tests│   └── test.yml    └── main.yml

4. Before proceeding further, the file is modified with group names called and which will contain the details of Load Balancer and Application servers respectively.

5. All the tasks which will be performed by playbook need to be updated in /etc/ansible/roles/ec2_instance/tasks/main.yml file. The below playbook tasks are uploaded which will create the security groups for load_balancer and application_servers, will launch the EC2 instances for load balancer (of count 1) and for hosting application (of count 2). Once it is done, the playbook will update the groups and mentioned in ansible hosts file with required details.

#instance.yml- name: Creation of security_group for Load_balancer

    description: "Security Group for load balancer"    access_key: "{{ access_key }}"
    secret_key: "{{ secret_key }}"

      - cidr_ip: "{{ cidr_ip }}"

    description: "Security Group for application server"
    vpc_id: "{{ vpc_id }}"
    access_key: "{{ access_key }}"
    secret_key: "{{ secret_key }}"

  amazon.aws.ec2_instance:
    name: "{{ app_instance }}"
    access_key: "{{ access_key }}"
    secret_key: "{{ secret_key }}"
    availability_zone: "{{ availability_zone }}"    image_id: "{{ image_id }}"
    instance_type: "{{ instance_type }}"
    key_name: "{{ key_name }}"    security_group: "{{ app_security_group_name }}"    wait: true    tags:    network:  register: app_server
- name: Update the host file with server details
  lineinfile:    line: "{{ load_balancer.instances[0].public_dns_name }} ansible_user='{{ ansible_user }}' ansible_ssh_private_key_file='{{ private_key_location }}'"
    insertafter: '^\[load_balancer\]'
    path: "/etc/ansible/hosts"

  lineinfile:    line: "{{ app_server.instances[0].public_dns_name }} ansible_user='{{ ansible_user }}' ansible_ssh_private_key_file='{{ private_key_location }}'"
    insertafter: '^\[app_server\]'
    path: "/etc/ansible/hosts"
- name: Update the host file with server details
  lineinfile:    line: "{{ app_server.instances[1].public_dns_name }} ansible_user='{{ ansible_user }}' ansible_ssh_private_key_file='{{ private_key_location }}'"
    insertafter: '^\[app_server\]'
    path: "/etc/ansible/hosts"

6. The variables need to be updated in /etc/ansible/roles/ec2_instance/vars/main.yml file as below.

image_id: "ami-071226ecf16aa7d96"instance_type: "t2.micro"lb_instance: "LOAD_BALANCER"
app_instance: "APP_INSTANCE"
assign_public_ip: truevpc_id: "vpc-96cc57ec"
